• T he estimated incidence of deep venous thrombosis (DVT)  
after moderate-to-severe TBI is 40%, and risk factors include  
advanced age, severe injury, prolonged immobilization, signifi-
cant fractures, and clotting disorder.
• R isk factors for spasticity development after TBI include more 
severe injury, motor dysfunction (hemiplegia or tetraplegia),  
associated anoxic injury, spinal cord injury, and age.
• Nor mal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) is a treatable neurosurgi -
cal complication following severe TBI, and it is typically  
associated with the triad of dementia, gait ataxia, and urinary  
incontinence, with gait disorder being most amenable to  
cerebral spinal fluid (CSF) shunting.
• P aroxysmal sympathetic hyperactivity (PSH), or “storming, ” is a 
complication of severe brain injury characterized by episodic 
surges of sympathetic and motor activity that are associated 
with signs and symptoms of hypertension, tachycardia, diapho -
resis, hyperthermia, tachypnea, rigidity, and dystonic posturing.
• Up t o 37% of TBI survivors have chronic anterior pituitary dys -
function, the diagnosis of which often indicates other neuroen -
docrine conditions, and this is associated with worse long-term 
outcome.
• A gitation is common during the acute phase of recovery from TBI, 
occurring in about a third of patients with moderate to severe TBI 
and poses many challenges to acute and rehabilitation care.
• Sleep -wake disorder management should begin with nonphar -
macologic intervention, including cognitive-behavioral therapy.
• F our diagnostic criteria were agreed upon to address the con -
cept of traumatic encephalopathy syndrome associated with 
chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE): (1) significant expo-
sure to repetitive head injuries; (2) core clinical features of  
cognitive impairment, neurobehavioral dysregulation, and a 
progressive course; (3) not fully accountable to another disor-
der; and (4) degree of functional impairment.
• TBI-r elated visual problems include diplopia, photophobia, 
tracking and fixation problems, and visuoperceptual complaints.
44
Traumatic Brain Injury
AMY K. WAGNER, KEVIN FRANZESE, AND JUSTIN L. WEPPNER
• H ypoxic ischemic brain injury (HIBI) occurs during both oxygen  
deprivation and reperfusion, and vulnerable structures associated 
with HIBI include the hippocampus, cerebellum, and basal ganglia.
• Blast tr aumatic brain injury (TBI) pathophysiology depends on 
energy and distance from the blast, protective equipment, num-
ber and timing of exposures, blast environment, and secondary 
effects beyond the overpressure injury that include penetrating 
trauma, being thrown by the blast, asphyxia, and toxic inhalants.
• Diag nosis of mild TBI requires a biomechanically plausible injury 
mechanism and either (1) observable neurologic deficits or men-
tal status changes directly linked to the injury, (2) the occurrence 
of more than two acute symptoms, or (3) at least one clinical or 
laboratory finding supporting the diagnosis of brain injury.
• L ess than 10% of mild TBI cases have positive computed to -
mography (CT) findings, and less than 1% require neurosurgical 
intervention.
• I ntracranial pressure (ICP) monitoring is recommended in all 
salvageable patients with severe TBI, including those with 
Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 3 to 8 and abnormal  
CT scans, as well as in patients with normal CT scans who  
are over 40, exhibit motor posturing, or have a systolic blood 
pressure below 90 mm Hg.
• P ost-TBI mortality and poor outcome are predicted using a  
core model of age, injury severity (motor subscale of the GCS), 
pupillary reactivity, and midline shift.
• F our primary disorders of consciousness (DoCs) are recognized: 
(1) coma; (2) unresponsive wakefulness syndrome; (3) minimally 
conscious state; and (4) posttraumatic confusional state.
• P osttraumatic seizures (PTS) can occur immediately ( ,24 hours 
after injury), early (24 hours to 7 days after injury), or late  
(.7 days after injury, posttraumatic epilepsy [PTE]).
• Hemipleg ic, spastic, traumatized, and immobilized joints tend 
to be more at risk for heterotopic ossification (HO) that most 
commonly involves the hips and elbows, followed by the  
shoulders and then the knees.
SUMMARY PEARLS
Definitions
Definition of Traumatic Brain Injury
T raumatic brain injury (TBI) is defined as “an alteration in brain 
function, or other evidence of brain pathology, caused by an 
external force.”
411 These types of injuries result from a jolt or blow 
to the head or from an object penetrating the skull and injuring 
the brain. Sources of TBI include motor vehicle accidents (MVAs), 
falls, assaults, and gunshot wounds. It is important to differentiate 
TBI from other types of acquired brain injuries, such as those 
caused by tumors or strokes, due to significant differences in 
mechanisms, treatments, and outcomes between traumatic and 
960

961Chapter 44  Traumatic Brain Injury
nontraumatic injuries. TBI should also be distinguished from the 
term head injury, which refers to a blow to the head or laceration 
that may occur without causing injury to the brain. A TBI can be 
classified as either open or closed. An open, or penetrating, TBI 
occurs when an object breaks the skull and enters the brain. A 
closed TBI occurs when the brain is injured but the skull remains 
intact. Diagnosis of TBI is often established through an evalua -
tion of clinical symptoms, along with positive neurologic signs 
and neuroimaging findings.
Defining Severity of Injury
TBI is categorized as mild, moderate, or severe. Historically, the 
Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)
590 is the gold standard for primary 
initial assessment of severity of injury based on level of conscious-
ness (Table 44.1).364 During the initial stages of diagnosis in the 
field or emergency facility, TBI is suspected or indicated if there 
has been a significant blow to the head and/or there is an altera-
tion or loss of consciousness at the time of injury. An initial GCS 
score is obtained through clinical evaluation. As indicated in  
Table 44.1, the score is obtained by rating the best visual, verbal, 
and motor responses. The total score is simply a sum of these rat-
ings, with scores ranging from 3 to 15. Coded notations may 
indicate that the total score, or specific subscale score, has been 
impacted by additional injury-related characteristics, such as 
medically induced chemical paralysis to facilitate intubation, sub-
stance intoxication, hypoxia, or other circumstances. Generally 
accepted guidelines identify three levels of severity based on GCS 
scores: mild (GCS 5 13–15), moderate (GCS 5 9–12), and 
severe (GCS 5 3–8). Individuals scoring in the mild TBI range 
should be evaluated at an emergency facility for monitoring but 
may require minimal or no hospitalization. For more severe inju-
ries involving loss of consciousness, the GCS is a widely accepted 
measure indicating the depth of coma and is generally serially 
measured until emergence from coma.
Recent discourse within the TBI community has focused on 
the topic of TBI classification, including the shortfalls associated 
with primary reliance on the GCS for designating injury severity 
and the use of imprecise terms such as “mild,” “moderate,” and 
“severe” injury given the heterogeneity and range of symptoms 
and long-term sequelae that can occur among survivors across this 
traditional injury severity spectrum as well as the limited prognos-
tic capacity of this measure.
447 A recent National Institutes of 
Health-sponsored TBI classification workshop suggested TBI 
classification can be improved with the utilization of blood-based 
biomarkers as well as imaging data.
447
Injury severity is also further characterized by the duration 
of loss of consciousness/coma and duration of posttraumatic 
amnesia (PTA). Individuals with mild TBI may experience brief 
loss of consciousness as well as transient confusion and other 
neurologic symptoms. They may also experience a brief period 
of PTA. PTA is generally characterized by a loss of memory for 
events surrounding the injury, disorientation, confusion, and 
significant cognitive impairment. Individuals sustaining mod -
erate-to-severe (ms)TBI have a more prolonged loss of con -
sciousness or coma. Upon emerging from coma, initially these 
individuals may respond inconsistently to environmental stim -
uli. As they become increasingly able to respond consistently, 
they may continue to exhibit symptoms of PTA for a significant 
period. Increased duration of coma and PTA have been associ -
ated with poorer prognosis and outcomes (discussed later in 
this chapter). Some individuals with severe injuries do not 
regain consciousness, remaining in unresponsive wakefulness 
syndrome (UWS) while others emerge only to a minimally 
conscious state (MCS).
Epidemiology
Epidemiology: United States and Worldwide
Approximately 2.8 million TBI-related emergency department 
(ED) visits, hospitalizations, and deaths occur each year in the 
United States.
589 Of these, approximately 2.5 million are TBI-
related ED visits, with the majority of these being mild injuries 
(often referred to as “concussions”), for which individuals are 
treated and released from the emergency room. The Centers for 
Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) reports approximately 
214,110 TBI-related hospitalizations in 2020 and 69,473 deaths 
from TBI in 2021.
604 Many individuals are left with long-term 
disability as a result of these injuries; according to one study, 598 
40% of individuals hospitalized as a result of TBI reported at 
least one ongoing issue the year following injury, with improve-
ment in memory and problem-solving listed as the most fre -
quently unmet needs.
120 Such cognitive impairments, in addition 
to physical and medical issues, have a significant impact on an 
individual’s ability to perform everyday activities. The CDC esti-
mates that at least 5.3 million Americans currently require long-
term or lifelong assistance with activities of daily living (ADLs) 
as a result of TBI.
115,574 Globally, TBI is a leading cause of death 
and disability, with a 2017 commission reporting that TBI is 
projected to continue being one of the leading three causes of 
injury-related death and disability through 2030. In Europe 
alone, over 2 million people are hospitalized due to TBI each 
year, with around 82,000 fatalities annually.
367 Incidence of TBI 
by age and sex vary slightly across regions but overall appear to 
follow patterns similar to those found in the United States.
275 For 
example, the distribution of mild, moderate, and severe TBI is 
consistent across the United States, Europe, Australia, and Asia: 
80% of TBIs are mild, and moderate and severe TBI each 
account for 10% of injuries.
580

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Epidemiology: Causes of Injury
In 2018, about 75% of nonfatal TBI hospitalizations resulted from 
either unintentional falls (51.0%) or MVAs (23.8%). The highest 
rates of nonfatal TBI hospitalizations due to unintentional falls were 
seen in adults aged 75 years and older (263.3 per 100,000 popula-
tion), followed by those aged 65 to 74 years (69.9 per 100,000 
population), and those aged 55 to 64 years (33.2 per 100,000 popu-
lation). For motor vehicle crashes, the highest hospitalization rates 
were among individuals aged 15 to 24 years (24.6 per 100,000 
population) and those aged 25 to 34 years (21.9 per 100,000 popu-
lation). Across all examined causes of nonfatal TBI hospitalizations, 
including motor vehicle crashes, falls, being struck by or against an 
object, self-harm, and assault, males consistently showed higher total 
estimates and age-adjusted rates compared to females.477
Associated Costs
A person who sustains an msTBI may require continuous care to 
aid in their recovery.118,605 The cost of inpatient hospital care for 
patients with TBI varies significantly, ranging from $2,130 to 
$401,808.612 The repercussions of a moderate or severe TBI extend 
beyond the individual, affecting their family and imposing signifi-
cant societal and economic burdens. The lifetime economic cost of 
TBI, encompassing both direct and indirect medical expenses, is 
estimated to be around $76.5 billion (adjusted to 2010 dollars).188 
On average, the cost per patient is approximately $87,634.612 Fur-
thermore, the expenses associated with fatal TBIs and those neces-
sitating hospitalization represent roughly 90% of the total medical 
costs for TBI. Although global figures are uncertain, the costs 
associated with TBI go well beyond the financial requirements for 
medical care. One must consider the societal costs associated with 
lost productivity for patients with TBI, their family members, or 
other caregivers. TBI also results in significant alterations in the 
roles and responsibilities for the injured individual as well as their 
family members or caregivers. Reduced participation in leisure or 
recreational activities is also a personal cost associated with TBI.
Epidemiology of Traumatic Brain Injury: 
Demographics/Risk Factors
Age and Gender
The two young age groups most at risk for sustaining a TBI are  
0 to 4 year olds and 15 to 19 year olds.589 MVAs result in the great-
est number of TBIs for people aged 15 to 19, while falls result in 
the greatest number of TBIs for 0 to 4 year olds.589 Nonfatal TBI 
hospitalization rates are highest among individuals aged 75 and 
older, at 321.4 per 100,000 people. Males also have a significant 
rate of 81.3 per 100,000, adjusted for age. The hospitalization rate 
for those aged 75 and older is about three times greater than for 
individuals aged 65 to 74, who had a rate of 105.5 per 100,000. 
Additionally, males experience nearly twice the rate of nonfatal 
TBI-related hospitalizations compared to females, who have a rate 
of 44.4 per 100,000, age-adjusted.477 As the population continues 
to age, age-specific considerations for treatment and management 
of those surviving their injuries will be of increasing importance.
Socioeconomic Status
Lower socioeconomic status (SES) in American cities correlates 
with an increased risk of injury, primarily as a result of the increased 
exposure to high-risk occupations, personal violence, older vehicles, 
and substandard housing.326 Additionally, road traffic injuries are 
highest in areas of low SES.560 Socioeconomic disadvantage is asso-
ciated with racial characteristics and rural locations; minority popu-
lations in urban areas are often of lower SES and have elevated risk 
for injury.326 According to data from 2018, the age-adjusted rates of 
nonfatal TBI-related hospitalizations were similar among different 
racial and ethnic groups. Non-Hispanic White individuals had a 
rate of 59.0 per 100,000 population, non-Hispanic Black individu-
als had a rate of 60.0 per 100,000, and Hispanic individuals had a 
rate of 59.6 per 100,000.477 Still, African Americans have a higher 
rate of TBI and associated mortality than other racial groups. 326 
Statistically speaking, rural populations have a greater incidence of 
behaviors relating to unhealthy lifestyles, including high alcohol 
consumption and psychosocial stress, in addition to being less likely 
to participate in preventative measures such as wearing seat 
belts.453,603 This increase in risk-taking behaviors is evident in terms 
of poorer health outcomes, specifically with injury-related out -
comes such as TBI.453,603 In urban areas, nearly half of all adoles -
cents who enter the institutional correction system have a history of 
TBI, with a calculated incidence of 3,107 TBIs per 100,000 per -
son/years.287 Previous studies demonstrate that over 60% of prison 
inmates self-report a previous history of TBI.664
Violence
Violence-related injuries account for 11% of TBI. 589 Violence-
related TBI can result from firearms, cutting instruments, blunt 
objects, or assaults such as pushing or hitting.212 Injury as a result 
of firearm use is the leading cause of death from TBI. 598 While 
74.2% of firearm-related TBIs are self-inflicted, 22.2% result 
from intentional assault, and 3.6% are unintentional.115 Between 
the year 2000 and 2019, there were 77,602 homicides involving 
TBIs caused by firearms. Firearms accounted for 68% of all TBI 
homicides. The study found that men, individuals residing in 
metropolitan areas, and non-Hispanic Black individuals experi -
enced higher rates of firearm-related TBI homicides. While the 
rate of TBI homicides not involving firearms decreased by 40%, 
the rate of those involving firearms saw only a 3% reduction dur-
ing the study period. From 2012/2013 to 2019, there was a sig -
nificant rise in firearm-related TBI homicides, particularly among 
women (20%) and residents of nonmetropolitan areas (39%). 
Firearm-related violence is a significant public health issue and is 
linked to the majority of TBI homicide deaths in the United 
States.645
According to a report from the CDC, approximately 12 million 
people experience rape, physical violence, or stalking by an intimate 
partner annually in the United States,54 with women as the majority 
of affected individuals.54,88,569 Over their lifetime, 24.3% of adult 
women and 13.8% of adult men experience severe physical violence 
by an intimate partner.54 Most cases of intimate partner violence 
(IPV) and domestic violence (DV) do not get reported; many who 
report IPV/DV are subject to repetitive injuries including both 
hypoxic-ischemic brain injury (HIBI) and TBI.291,292 Of IPV and 
DV cases presenting to care and captured via the National T rauma 
Data Bank, 31.1% involved TBI. Skull fractures were the most com-
mon cranial injury, followed by various types of hemorrhages, con-
tusions, and cerebral edema. Most IPV/DV-related TBIs were mild.   
Compared to non-TBI cases, TBI cases involved more female 
patients, more Black patients, higher intensive care unit (ICU) 
admissions, increased mortality, longer hospital stays, and a lower 
likelihood of discharge home.499

963Chapter 44  Traumatic Brain Injury
Psychosocial Factors
Higher incidence of TBI for young males may be associated with 
a tendency to engage in high-risk behaviors. In addition, sub -
stance use is related to TBI incidence; up to 50% of individuals 
injured test positive for alcohol or other substances at the time of 
injury.
116 Prior psychiatric history is a risk factor for TBI, particu-
larly with those who have a history of anxiety, depression, and 
conduct disorder.
613
Military Traumatic Brain Injury  
Epidemiology
Members of the U.S. military are an evolving group at risk for 
sustaining TBI. According to the Defense and Veterans Brain 
Injury Center (DVBIC), 499,852 TBIs were reported among 
U.S. service members globally from 2000 to late 2023.
421 Addi-
tionally, over 185,000 veterans receiving U.S. Department of 
Veterans Affairs health care have been diagnosed with at least one 
TBI.
607 Military members who sustain a TBI are more likely to 
be male because men represent a larger percentage of the military 
service population (85%).
478 In the military population, approx-
imately 82.2% of TBIs are classified as mild injuries while 12.5% 
are moderate-to-severe injuries.
421
Compared with the civilian population, the Department of 
Defense reports that the leading causes of military TBIs include 
blast injury (72%), falls (11%), vehicular incidents (6%), injuries 
due to fragments (5%), and other injuries (6%).
190 Blast-related 
injuries can be defined as “blast-plus” as service members are often 
involved in more than one type of injury at a time, such as an 
explosive attack followed by a vehicular crash. The nature of inju-
ries in military personnel, such as concurrent battle wounds and 
brain injury conditions, often results in comorbidities with blast 
(b)TBI. Research is ongoing to distinguish the effects of blast 
overpressure from those of blunt trauma in these injuries.
75 In 
part, due to the propensity for repetitive head trauma 152 and 
increased allostatic burden 459 associated with active military ser -
vice, common comorbid and preexisting conditions include 
PTSD, depression, anxiety, sleep disorders, attention disorders, 
and cognitive impairments.
480 In the military population, repeated 
exposure to repetitive head injuries, along with the physical and 
psychological injuries associated with military service, accumulate 
over a person's lifetime. This accumulation increases the risk of 
negative outcomes and a high incidence of co-occurring condi -
tions.
68 These comorbidities, such as PTSD, are notably expensive 
to treat. In 2008, the RAND Corporation estimated that the 
baseline cost to treat PTSD alone for 50,000 enlisted-5 paygrade 
service members was $204,691,652 when including lives lost to 
suicide and $119,829,381 when excluding those lives. The esti -
mated total cost to treat deployment-related TBI ranged from 
$554 million to $854 million, with 47% to 57% of that cost 
attributed to productivity loss.
480,585
Epidemiology of Traumatic Brain Injury: 
Demographics/Risk Factors (Global)
Global statistics suggest that low- to middle-income countries have 
a greater number of risk factors that contribute to military-related 
TBI.
14 Risk factors vary by country and may depend on the inci -
dence of factors such as poor road design, substandard vehicles, 
higher rates of violence due to war, and fewer prevention mea -
sures.275 Low- to middle-income countries also have inadequate 
health systems to address TBI-related care.14,275 Accurate informa-
tion on the demographics and etiology of TBI in low- to middle-
income countries is especially difficult to obtain. Despite this  
lack of information, it is clear that the burden of TBI is evident 
worldwide.
Pathophysiology Associated with  
Traumatic Brain Injury
The pathophysiologic processes associated with TBI are com-
plex. They consist of (1) a primary injury that disrupts brain 
tissue and function at impact; (2) secondary injury through 
multiple biochemical cascades that propagate cellular dysfunc-
tion and lead to cell death; and (3) a chronic degeneration, 
repair, and regeneration process that occurs over the long term 
after the injury has occurred. Fig. 44.1 depicts the continua-
tion of injury and repair associated with pathophysiology after 
TBI.
Primary Injury
TBI occurs in conjunction with mechanical forces that cause 
disruption to the brain tissue. Closed head injuries can occur due 
to mechanical forces, which shear axons, as well as forces at the 
site of the impact or at the point opposite the impact.
207 Contact 
forces occur when the head is prevented from moving after it is 
struck. Inertial forces occur when the head is set into motion and 
results in acceleration and subsequent deceleration.
245 Inertial 
forces associated with angular acceleration result in diffuse axonal 
injury (DAI), a process that causes tensile strains resulting in 
microscopic disruption of axons, cerebral edema, and neuronal 
disconnection. In angular acceleration, the brain’s center of grav-
ity moves over a center of angulation, or fulcrum, located in the 
lower-to-middle cervical region.
210 The severity of DAI depends 
on the duration, magnitude, and direction of the angular accel -
eration, as well as an associated impact.210 In severe cases of DAI, 
more than just superficial axons are affected and injuries affect

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
deeper white matter structures. The gray-white matter junction is 
also particularly vulnerable to DAI. Midline brain structures, 
such as the corpus callosum, are often impacted by DAI, and 
DAI is also associated with loss of consciousness and coma. 
Recovery from DAI is gradual and can be linked to the duration 
of coma.245 Violent shaking associated with child abuse generates 
significant acceleration/deacceleration forces, which result in 
shearing injury to brain tissue, disruption of blood vessels, and 
retinal bleeding.64,192
Inertial forces associated with translational acceleration result in 
a head movement that is in line with the brain’s center of gravity. 
The resulting differential movement of the brain relative to the 
skull causes focal injuries such as contusions. Cortical contusions 
often occur on gyral crests, particularly on the undersurface of the 
frontal and anterior temporal lobes, where bony prominences 
located in the basilar skull can create tissue and vascular disrup -
tion. Contusions may occur under the impact site (coup injury) 
and result from a rapid change in skull distortion during impact.210 
Contusions remote from the injury site and opposite to the impact 
are contra-coup injuries, which occur due to negative pressure 
generated from the impact associated with translational accelera -
tion.462 Patients with significant TBI may have one or more contu-
sions, and deficits observed with cerebral contusions are linked 
with the lesion location and size.
DAI and focal contusions can result in neuronal disconnec -
tion, or diaschisis. First proposed by Constantin von Monakow in 
1905, the concept of diaschisis refers to neurons remote from a 
site of injury but anatomically connected to the damaged area 
being functionally depressed. Because every structure in the brain 
is directly or indirectly connected to all other structures, the basic 
idea generated from this concept is that damage to one structure 
will be disruptive to the function of other structures. Imaging 
studies suggest that diaschisis can result in the metabolic depres -
sion of areas both regionally associated and remote from the site 
of injury. 180,415 Others suggest that focal cortical lesions may 
impact contralateral cortical functioning by way of intercallosal 
connections 149 and can impact the function of subcortical struc -
tures including the striatum. 180 Resolution of diaschisis is one of 
several theories for recovery of function 180,415 discussed later in 
this chapter.
Epidural hematomas (EDHs) result from local impact and 
subsequent laceration of underlying dural veins and arteries. The 
middle meningeal artery is commonly the source of EDHs, and 
damage to dural sinuses can also cause EDH. When an EDH 
develops from a disrupted artery, a neurologic emergency occurs 
as the EDH quickly expands and rapidly causes neurologic dete -
rioration.245 Subdural hematomas (SDHs) result from inertial 
forces and the tearing of bridging veins. 462 Bridging veins are 
susceptible to shear and rupture from brief high velocity angular 
accelerations associated with falls. 211 T raumatic subarachnoid 
hemorrhage (SAH) occurs when angular acceleration shears ves -
sels located in the subarachnoid space.245
Secondary Injury
Secondary injury develops over the minutes, hours, and days fol -
lowing the initial impact and is associated with disruption of 
cerebral blood flow and metabolism, massive release of neuro -
chemicals, cerebral edema, and disruption of ion homeostasis 
leading to cellular injury and eventual cell death. Much of what is 
known about secondary injury is derived from postmortem analy-
ses of human brain tissues or the analysis of blood, cerebrospinal 
fluid (CSF), and parenchymal microdialysate fluid obtained early 
in the clinical course after TBI. Additionally, much work explor -
ing secondary injury has utilized in vivo experimental models of 
TBI and in vitro injury methods. Experimental models of TBI 
produce one or more types of primary injury, which leads to the 
development of secondary injury cascades that can resemble 
observations made in the human condition. 148,323,378,407,465,559
Brain swelling occurs in response to the initial injury and 
early events involved with secondary injury. Swelling results in 
elevated intracranial pressure (ICP) and decreased cerebral perfu -
sion pressure (CPP). If severe enough, brain swelling can lead to 
herniation, which has potentially fatal consequences. In some 
cases, elevated ICP is due to the development of focal extra axial 
lesions like SDH and EDH described above. In other cases, ele -
vated ICP and intracranial hypertension result from global 
mechanisms occurring on a cellular level that lead to brain 
edema. Some mechanisms that impact brain edema include  
vasogenic edema, increased tissue osmolarity, and vascular dys -
regulation resulting from increased cerebral blood volume. How -
ever, brain edema due to neuronal swelling and astrocyte swelling 
secondary to increased glutamate metabolism after excitotoxic 
insult may impart the most damage. 316
Excitatory amino acids (EAAs) like glutamate and aspartate are 
ubiquitous and important neurotransmitters for normal brain 
function. After TBI, excess EAA levels are present in extracellular 
microdialysate fluid as well as in CSF . 77,626 Excess EAA release 
occurs, in part, from the mechanical stresses associated with the 
stretch injury of axons. Elevations in extracellular glutamate levels 
after TBI contribute to excitotoxic injury by affecting other paths 
of secondary head injury through two primary mechanisms. By 
binding their target receptors, excess EAAs can trigger an influx of 
sodium and chloride that leads to acute neuronal and astrocytic 
swelling. Excess EAA levels can also lead to an intracellular cal -
cium (Ca11 ) influx as well as the release of Ca 11  from intra-
cellular stores, leading to delayed cellular damage or death. 101,570 
EAA-mediated, Ca 11 -dependent production of nitric oxide, 
superoxide, and free radical damage to DNA and cellular  
membranes lead to cell death. 358 Regional differences in the  
distribution of EAA-sensitive receptors contributes to selective 
vulnerability to excitotoxic injury of some regions including the 
hippocampus, a critical region for learning and memory.423
Lactate is the byproduct of glycolysis, and in neurons, lactate is 
converted to pyruvate for oxidative metabolism. Lactate/pyruvate 
(L/P) ratios reflect the cellular energy balance and cytosolic  
oxidation-reduction state. 554 Increased metabolic demand after 
TBI is due, in part, to increased astrocytic glutamate uptake and 
results in increased lactate. Reduced cerebral blood flow, includ -
ing pericontusional hypoperfusion, has been documented in both 
in vivo and in vitro models 255 and in humans 565 early after TBI 
and results in ischemic injury. Elevations in L/P ratios have been 
measured in pericontusional tissue in humans 615 and are associ -
ated with other aspects of secondary injury, including increased 
ICP and mitochondrial dysfunction.
Although extracellular lactate has classically been a marker for 
anaerobic metabolism with HIBI, research also indicates that lac-
tate is utilized by brain tissue during an insult as an important 
energy source in oxidative metabolism. 505 Evidence supports a 
dichotomous role of lactate in which it is produced by the astro -
cyte via glycolytic metabolism, to be directly consumed by the 
neuron after uptake through lactate transporters.131,505 Physiologi-
cally,474 and after TBI,505 EAAs are likely triggers to this metabolic 
process. After injury, central nervous system (CNS) estradiol

facilitates lactate production for neuronal utilization by modulat-
ing lactate transporters.409 Interestingly, experimental studies sug-
gest that lactate is used as a neuronal energy substrate after TBI, 
and lactate administration after TBI is neuroprotective and 
decreases cognitive deficits.506
Mitochondrial dysfunction is often the result of energy failure 
and excitotoxic insult and results in the formation of highly reac -
tive free radicals and oxidative damage to cell membranes and 
DNA. Hydroxyl radicals and other reactive oxygen species (ROS) 
react with many molecules found in living cells, including DNA, 
membrane lipids, and carbohydrates. CNS tissue is particularly 
vulnerable to oxidative injury due to its naturally high rate of 
oxidative metabolic activity, the nonreplicating nature of neurons, 
high levels of transition metals, and a high membrane-to-  
cytoplasm ratio in neurons. 18 Superoxide radicals are a principal 
mediator of the microvascular damage after TBI. 332 Free-reactive 
iron generated from hemorrhage catalyzes the formation of free 
radicals.316 ROS production may also be a downstream effect of 
caspase-mediated cell death (apoptosis).187
Cell death in CNS tissues after TBI falls into two primary 
categories: (1) cellular necrosis and (2) apoptosis or programmed 
cell death. Necrotic cells display cellular and nuclear swelling as 
well as disruption of cellular membranes. In contrast, apoptotic 
cells are characterized by cellular shrinkage, nuclear condensation, 
and DNA fragmentation. 298 Apoptosis is accompanied by a cas -
cade of events required for cell death to occur. 566 In TBI, cell 
death patterns often reflect a combination of both necrosis and 
apoptosis.
Apoptosis can be initiated through either extracellular or intra-
cellular signaling pathways. Cysteine-aspartic acid proteases (cas -
pases) play an essential role in cell death via both pathways. 
Extracellular binding of cell surface death receptors, including the 
tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF- a) receptor and FAS ligand 
receptor, and initiation of apoptotic signals—including caspase-8 
and caspase-3—occur within seconds of ligand binding. Intracel -
lular signaling is initiated by mitochondrial dysfunction in 
response to disturbances in cellular energy balance, oxidative 
injury, and changes in ionic homeostasis. This mitochondrial 
stress leads to the opening of the mitochondrial transition pore 
and cytochrome-C release.
Release of cytosolic cytochrome-C induces other caspases—
including caspase-9 and caspase-3—which leads to the cleavage of 
key cellular elements like cytoskeletal proteins and DNA repair 
proteins as well as the activation of endonucleases. 109 In addition 
to this intracellular caspase-dependent process for apoptosis, 
intracellular apoptosis signaling can also occur via a caspase-
independent pathway with apoptosis-inducing factor (AIF). AIF 
is released from mitochondria and results in DNA fragmentation. 
The mitochondria houses both prosurvival and prodeath proteins 
that are both members of the BCL-2 protein family, 7 each of 
which can be manipulated experimentally to affect cell survival. 
As an example, estrogen enhances BCL-2 expression and cell sur-
vival after CNS injury.274
Inflammation after TBI represents very complex humoral sig -
naling cascades that include cytokines, chemokines, soluble adhe-
sion molecules, and immune cell-derived soluble inflammatory 
receptors that communicate with resident and infiltrating inflam-
matory cells. Cytokines—including interleukin 1beta (IL-1 b) 
and TNF- a—are central to propagating the inflammatory 
response, as well as promoting neurotoxicity and edema via exci -
totoxicity and oxidative injury. 622 Inflammatory molecules are 
synthesized by peripheral cells as well as CNS neurons and glia 
and are increased early after injury. IL-1 b has potent leukocyte 
signaling properties that lead to cellular inflammatory responses 
after TBI, and IL-1b and other inflammatory mediators like IL-6 
can facilitate blood–brain barrier (BBB) disruption and transport  
peripheral inflammatory components into the CNS. Activated 
microglia synthesize multiple inflammatory mediators in the 
CNS. Specifically, IL-1 b and TNF- a can mediate synthesis of 
neurotoxic compounds like arachidonic acid and are implicated 
in excitotoxic components of secondary injury.521
The innate systemic immune response is perpetuated by both 
the sympathetic and parasympathetic autonomic nervous systems, 
which drive activation and mobilization of both cellular and 
humoral immunity.254 Similar to sepsis,611,649 injury-induced sys-
temic activation of the immune system is followed by a period of 
immunosuppression that results in lymphopenia, neutrophil 
abnormalities, and a compensatory antiinflammatory response 
that leads to lymphopenia and leukocytosis when severe. 254,359,535 
350 Suppressed immune responses after injury can facilitate infec -
tions and impair wound healing, negatively impacting neurologic 
recovery.220,350,438
In addition to the systemic immune response, constitutive 
microglia and cytokine production in the brain promotes neuronal 
differentiation and survival under normal conditions. Cytokines 
play a dual role in secondary injury with TBI—the inflammatory 
cascades may either enhance cellular injury or provide neuropro -
tection. Some studies suggest that cytokine effects may be dose-
dependent, with higher levels more likely to be associated with 
detrimental effects and lower levels neuroprotective.145,563,564 Con-
sistent with this previous work, recent clinical evidence implicates 
CNS cortisol as a regulator of this dualistic role after TBI.533 Also, 
some cytokines support neuroprotection and neurorepair through 
their effects on neurotrophin production, excitotoxicity, antiin -
flammatory properties, and autoimmunity response. 11,308,432,617 
CNS inflammatory biomarker profiles can be influenced by extra-
cerebral injury wherein major trauma itself can elicit a significant 
systemic inflammatory response.335
Evidence suggests that neuroinflammation can continue for 
months and years after injury, adversely contributing to outcome 
and implicating TBI as triggering a chronic inflammatory 
state.333,500,552 Specific cytokine effects in the setting of long-term 
or chronic inflammation remain largely unstudied; however, some 
evidence suggests beneficial effects can occur at delayed or chronic 
time points after injury. 433 For example, time-dependent func -
tions have been demonstrated in TNF- a,539 with higher expres -
sion being protective at later time points after injury.
Aside from B-cell lymphoma 2 (Bcl-2) and the prosurvival 
components of neuroinflammation, other CNS compounds can 
serve as endogenous neuroprotectants after TBI. Adenosine is a 
purine ribonucleoside and is ubiquitous in the brain, where it 
largely functions as a major neuroinhibitory molecule. After 
experimental brain injury, extracellular adenosine levels increase 
by 50- to 100-fold when compared to baseline, 48 a phenomenon 
that is implicated as an endogenous neuroprotective mechanism. 
Adenosine functions as a neuroinhibitory molecule through its 
primary target, the A1 receptor. A1 receptor activation is a key 
local regulator of EAA release during ischemia and trauma. 21 
Adenosine can also hyperpolarize neuronal membranes and atten-
uate intracellular Ca11  accumulation.
Neurotrophins may also have a neuroprotective role after TBI, 
particularly because early increases in neurotrophin expression 
have been reported in experimental TBI. 262,682 The downstream 
effects of neurotrophins like brain-derived neurotrophic factor

(BDNF) may be variable and dependent upon the regional target 
receptor milieu. Notably, increases in the proapoptotic target 
receptor p75 are observed with acute CNS insult and with 
increased age.
123,520 Thus BDNF’s role in TBI recovery and mor-
tality may be dependent on the relative balance of these target 
receptors. In support of this concept, recent clinical reports impli-
cate BDNF in mortality after TBI, where there were unique 
relationships between BDNF genetics, acute BDNF levels, older 
age, and mortality risk.
169-171 Early evidence also suggests a pos -
sible protective role for chronic BDNF levels during recovery.170
Concomitant Injury
TBIs, particularly more severe cases, often are not isolated inju -
ries. High-speed vehicular crashes as well as active combat fre -
quently result in multiple injuries that can affect outcome. 315 In 
civilians, concomitant injuries are linked with more disability 
even 1 year after the injury.
628 TBIs associated with multiple other 
injuries are also associated with longer acute care lengths of stay 
and need for rehabilitation resources.
627 In addition to increased 
functional deficits, rehabilitation needs, and more disability, con-
comitant injuries can directly impact secondary injury and associ-
ated pathology and have received some attention in the literature. 
For example, experimental models incorporating TBI and hemor-
rhagic shock show increased hippocampal injury compared  
to TBI alone, a phenomenon likely associated with increased  
secondary ischemia.
137
Military Blast Injury
Although the military conflicts in Iraq and Afghanistan have 
ended, TBI remains a significant concern for veterans and active-
duty military personnel who have served in these regions. The 
frequent use of improvised explosive devices (IEDs) in these con-
flicts has resulted in many military personnel sustaining TBI from 
blast injury. The effects of a bomb blast on the brain depend on 
several factors including the energy, distance from the blast, body 
position in relation to the blast, use of protective equipment, 
number of blast exposures, and time interval between blast expo -
sures. The environment is another important factor. Blast expo -
sures in a closed space usually result in a more severe injury than 
those occurring in an open environment.
200,669 Blast injury can 
result in both blunt and penetrating trauma and follows a specific 
nomenclature (Box 44.1 and Fig. 44.2). Explosives can be catego-
rized as high-order explosives (HEs) or low-order explosives 
(LEs), and each can cause a distinct injury pattern.
HEs produce a supersonic over pressurization shock wave
606 that 
is associated with “primary injury.” Perforation of the tympanic 
membrane is the most common physical sign following blast 
trauma, with some studies reporting tympanum injury as a sensitive 
screening test for the presence of primary blast injury.
678 In addition 
to middle ear damage, brain injury can occur from a primary blast 
injury without overt evidence of injury. The rapid pressure changes 
generated from the overpressure wave result in shear and stress 
forces that lead to trauma such as concussion, SDH, and 
DAI.
41,244,578 Penetrating head injury from flying debris is the result 
of secondary blast injury. Both HEs and LEs can result in tertiary 
blast injury from being physically thrown by a blast wind into the 
ground or other objects. Quaternary injury includes asphyxia or 
inhalation of toxic chemicals that may also contribute to brain 
injury. Many individuals who suffer from blast-induced TBI sustain 
their injury from more than one component of the blast.
Increasingly, research is being conducted to develop relevant 
blast injury models of TBI in order to examine and characterize 
the mechanisms of primary and secondary injury and to assess 
behavioral effects.
41,89,357 Clinically, symptoms after bTBI of any 
severity can include headache, insomnia, decreased memory and 
attention, cognitive deficits, irritability, and depression.
203 Addi-
tional research is focused on clinical symptom screening and 
outcomes as well as the physiologic markers and biomarkers asso-
ciated with this type of TBI.
9,574,600 A more recent focus is on the 
effects of repetitive blast injury exposure and the development of 
neurodegenerative disorders and also the added comorbidity of

PTSD.63,403,614,684 High lifetime blast exposure is associated with 
diminished overall neurobehavioral functioning following mild 
TBI.342 Repeated exposure to blasts throughout a military career 
adversely affects the recovery process from TBI. Such exposure is 
linked to heightened neurobehavioral issues and posttraumatic 
stress symptoms after a TBI. 37 The polytrauma clinical triad 
includes chronic pain, PTSD, and postconcussion syndrome; 
these conditions rarely occur in isolation after blast TBI. Studies 
have highlighted the importance of treating the totality of comor-
bidities when providing care for blast-injured military service 
members, as opposed to treating a single disorder in isola -
tion.107,496,519 A recent observational study demonstrates the  
transition from military to civilian life, which can be especially 
difficult for veterans with a history of bTBI. 114
Hypoxic and Hypoxic-Ischemic Brain Injury
The brain’s high metabolic demand puts it at special risk for insult 
in the event of insufficient perfusion or oxygenation, leading to 
HIBI or hypoxic brain injury (HBI), respectively.79 HBI can occur 
with severe hypotension such as that associated with respiratory 
failure, hypoxia such as in carbon monoxide poisoning, or septic 
shock.79 HIBI can also occur from multiple etiologies, including 
spontaneous cardiac arrest (SCA). 79 SCA survivors with brain 
injury are becoming more common with the now widespread 
application of treatments like targeted temperature management 
and hypothermia after cardiac arrest.155 In general, recovery from 
HIBI is considered to be more limited than recovery from 
TBI,125,126,558 and individuals with HIBI are often at risk for many 
of the same complications observed with TBI. 61,372,439 Although 
recovery from HIBI is generally more limited than with TBI, 
meaningful recovery can occur among severely injured HIBI sur -
vivors, including those with disorders of consciousness (DoC).167,659
The pathology associated with global HIBI differs significantly 
from regional (e.g., inferior frontal lobes) and mechanical injury 
mechanisms associated with TBI, where injury occurs during 
initial deprivation of oxygen and then during reperfusion. 531 
Especially vulnerable structures associated with HIBI include the 
hippocampus, cerebellum, and basal ganglia. 79 The “watershed” 
regions of the brain most distant from the major blood vessels are 
also vulnerable to HIBI due to their location rather than meta -
bolic characteristics.79 Ischemic vulnerability specifically leads to 
dysfunctional basal ganglia dopamine (DA)455 neurotransmission 
as well as cerebellar and brainstem damage, and it may contribute 
to oculomotor dysfunction, movement disorders, and other  
cognitive-behavioral deficits, similar to meso-circuit dysfunc -
tion.22,485,513 Sequelae of HIBI can include DoC, seizures, cogni -
tive impairment, cortical blindness, movement disorders (i.e., 
Lance-Adams syndrome, myoclonus, tremor, and dystonia), par -
oxysmal sympathetic hyperactivity (PSH), and mood prob -
lems.20,531 Delayed posthypoxic leukoencephalopathy is a severe 
and potentially fatal late complication of HIBI where patients 
initially do well, but then up to 10 days postarrest show a marked 
neurologic decline with radiographic evidence of widespread cor -
tical demyelination.79
It is recommended that efforts at prognostication be under -
taken among cardiac arrest survivors who do not regain con -
sciousness after rewarming and stabilization. These individuals 
should undergo a comprehensive neurologic exam, imaging of the 
brain, electroencephalogram (EEG), evoked potentials, and mea -
surement of neuron-specific enolase (NSE). T wo or more unfavor-
able results would suggest a poor outcome, including absent 
pupillary and corneal reflexes at 72 hours, bilaterally absent N20 
waves on somatosensory evoked potential (SSEP), unfavorable 
EEG background or status myoclonus, high NSE, and evidence 
of anoxic injury on brain imaging. 434,531
Neurotransmission Repair,  
Regeneration, and Recovery
Acute mechanisms of secondary injury heavily influence the 
degree and type of injury and deficits that individuals experience 
with TBI. The chronic period after injury is characterized by mul-
tiple neurotransmitter deficits and cellular dysfunction. During 
this period, however, research demonstrates that the brain is ame-
nable to neuroplasticity, repair, and recovery, and the potential for 
these processes can be augmented using relevant rehabilitation 
strategies.
One mechanism by which TBI recovery occurs is through the 
reversal of diaschisis. Contemporary thought on the concept sug-
gests that the functional effects of diaschisis can be either revers -
ible or permanent. However, the mechanisms by which diaschisis 
resolves are poorly understood and likely multifactorial. The reso-
lution of cerebral edema and blood flow regulation may contrib -
ute to early recovery. 318 Later, the alleviation of diaschisis may 
involve factors such as synaptic plasticity, 32,415 axonal sprouting, 
and cortical reorganization.319,677 Diaschisis after brain injury can 
be manipulated in experimental models via behavioral experience 
(rehabilitation), nonpharmacologic intervention, and pharmaco -
logic intervention 95,369,410,445,458 including neurostimulants such 
as amphetamine.181
Experimental and clinical research suggest that DA systems are 
key pathways involved in attention, task salience, and cognition, 
and these pathways are chronically impaired after TBI. 38 Altera-
tions in both presynaptic and postsynaptic dopaminergic proteins 
in the striatum and frontal cortex have been documented, includ-
ing important proteins involved in DA synthesis and upt
ake.38,625,629,639 Decreases in striatal dopamine transporter (DAT) 
expression, a key protein in regulating the extracellular half-life of 
DA, have been documented in humans. 154,279 Further research 
demonstrates that striatal neurotransmission is impaired and may 
be the result of TBI-induced changes in DA protein functional -
ity.638,639 Clinical research also suggests impaired striatal DA 
transmission, including reduced DAT expression profiles that 
mirror experimental modeling data (described below) and that are 
both DAT and D2 genotype specific.637
Noradrenergic systems affect arousal, sleep-wake cycles, vigi -
lance, and cognition, and the norepinephrine (NE) transporter 
appears to affect noradrenergic neurotransmission as well as inter-
acting with DA systems to impact DA neurotransmission. 650 
Noradrenergic function is impaired early after TBI. Previous 
reports suggest that cortical NE levels are decreased within 
24  hours following brain injury, and adrenergic receptors are 
altered after injury.492,493 Research shows that NE augmentation 
early after TBI is neuroprotective, and NE agonists aid in neuro -
recovery.62,158 Importantly, NE is critical for governing cortical 
plasticity and facilitating recovery post-TBI. By stimulating 
adrenergic systems with amphetamine and L-dihydroxyphenylser-
ine (L-DOPS), a precursor of NE, neuronal damage after TBI can 
be minimized and sprouting is promoted.225,302,568,573
Cholinergic systems are important for memory and cognition, 
and evidence suggests chronic impairments occur after TBI. Hip-
pocampal expression of the vesicular acetylcholine transporter is

increased; however, evoked release of acetylcholine in the neocor-
tex and hippocampus is reduced following experimental TBI.147,548 
Chronic changes in cholinergic receptor expression patterns and 
affinities have also been noted in experimental TBI models. 136,362 
However, donepezil has been shown in preclinical studies to 
enhance neurogenesis, reduce neuroinflammation, and improve 
behavioral recovery.687 Further, a recent multicenter randomized 
clinical trial (RCT) comparing donepezil and placebo showed 
that daily treatment with donepezil for 10 weeks improves verbal 
learning and memory after TBI.24
In addition to improved protection and cholinergic function 
acutely, other studies support a role for neurotrophin administra-
tion in promoting long-term recovery. Despite acute increases in 
neurotrophin production, other work suggests that hippocampal 
brain BDNF is decreased during chronic recovery from experi -
mental TBI. 98 An enriched environment can reverse these 
decreases98; however, chronic pericontusional administration of 
BDNF does not improve behavioral outcomes or hippocampal 
cell survival.55 Exercise is also a powerful tool for boosting BDNF 
levels that has shown some promise in preclinical studies in 
facilitating recovery when initiated not too early after injury102,235 
and also when initiated preinjury. 320,695 Studies suggest that 
BDNF target receptor expression ratios can vary following isch -
emia and TBI, such that BDNF signaling can promote apoptotic 
pathways in the acute phase following experimental TBI. 520 
BDNF’s early role in TBI recovery may be dependent on the rela-
tive balance of these target receptors. Recent work suggests 
genetic variation in the BDNF gene may be related to acute 
BDNF levels as well as mortality in an age-dependent manner 
following TBI.170,171 With these studies, the research suggests that 
neurotrophins may be manipulated strategically to improve recov-
ery in some with TBI, and this recovery may be secondary to 
stimulation of brain repair and neuroplasticity.
Principles of Experimental Neurotransmitter 
Dysfunction and Treatment After Traumatic 
Brain Injury
The neurobiology of catecholaminergic dysfunction, particularly 
DA dysfunction, is the most characterized in the experimental 
literature.38 Early changes in DA levels occur after experimental 
TBI.382 Chronic DA receptor and synthetic protein changes after 
TBI have also been characterized using experimental TBI models. 
The DAT is a key protein regulating the extracellular half-life of 
DA in the synaptic cleft and the therapeutic target of neurostimu-
lants like methylphenidate (MPH) and amphetamine. Long-term 
changes in striatal and cortical DAT expression and intracellular 
trafficking have been identified 625,639 as gender-specific. 624 Fur -
ther, functional reductions in DAT after experimental TBI are 
associated with reduced striatal neurotransmission. 625,638 Other 
changes in postsynaptic secondary signaling mechanisms, as well 
as changes in presynaptic DA synthetic enzyme levels, have been 
identified.38 These findings are in contrast to abnormally elevated 
striatal DA neurotransmission and DAT protein observed 2 weeks 
after insult in an experimental model of cardiac arrest–associated 
HIBI455 and suggest that DA agents may need to be avoided dur-
ing the postacute phases after HIBI.
Behavioral pharmacology studies show that daily treatment 
with the DAT inhibitor MPH has a beneficial impact on cogni -
tion after experimental TBI. 312 Previous work suggests that the 
cognitive benefits of MPH are more pronounced in males 629 and 
that daily MPH therapy has a neurorestorative effect on striatal 
neurotransmission in males through MPH-mediated changes in 
DAT function.625,638 Previous clinical trial evidence of daily treat-
ment with the pleiotropic DA enhancer amantadine 150 supports 
amantadine use in severely injured individuals in the postacute 
and early chronic recovery phases after TBI.216 Experimental TBI 
data also show that the D2 receptor agonist bromocriptine can 
benefit learning and memory. 310 Interestingly, early treatment 
with bromocriptine also appears to decrease oxidative damage 
initiated by the injury.309
Evidence also suggests that NE systems are disrupted after 
experimental TBI but can be manipulated to improve recovery. 
Cortical NE levels are decreased in the first 24 hours after 
injury,492 and NE turnover is impaired chronically after injury.202 
Further, a1-adrenergic receptor expression is decreased. 493 Local 
NE infusions early after injury enhance recovery, 62 and stimulat-
ing adrenergic systems with amphetamine can decrease neuronal 
damage after TBI.224 Previous research suggests that at least some 
of the beneficial effects of amphetamine may be due to its ability 
to increase neurotrophin production after injury. 235 Daily treat -
ment with low doses of the selective NE transporter (NET) 
inhibitor, atomoxetine, benefits cognitive recovery after experi -
mental TBI.504
Experimental TBI studies also demonstrate decreased  
hippocampal cholinergic transmission 147 147and alterations in 
cholinergic synthetic and metabolic enzyme production. 99,151 For 
example, a single dose of the acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, riv -
astigmine, early after experimental TBI improves cerebral edema, 
cognition, and motor functioning. 99 Interestingly, daily treat -
ment with cytidine 5´-diphospho (CDP)-choline, a key interme -
diary in the biosynthesis of phosphatidylcholine, improves  
cognitive recovery in experimental TBI and increases hippocam -
pal and cortical acetylcholine levels.151 However, beneficial effects 
of CDP-choline did not translate in a prior large, multisite  
clinical trial involving acute and chronic administration in indi -
viduals with complicated msTBI. 690
Evaluation and Treatment of Traumatic  
Brain Injury
Management of Mild Traumatic Brain  
Injury (Concussion)
Even mild TBI, or concussion, may result in significant alterations 
to brain physiology. After an initial increase in glucose use, brain 
metabolism is impaired and develops a state of energy crisis and 
oxidative injury and initiates biochemical cell death pathways. 
These derangements can occur for a number of weeks after the 
concussion and persist even with a normal GCS and general neu-
rologic exam.219 Per the 2023 American Congress of Rehabilita -
tion Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Mild TBI,556 a mild TBI is 
diagnosed when there is a biomechanically plausible mechanism 
of injury that aligns with forces known to cause brain injury.  
Following this, one or more specific criteria must be met. These 
criteria include the presence of one or more clinical signs attribut-
able to brain injury, such as observable neurologic deficits or 
changes in mental status directly linked to the injury. Addition -
ally, the occurrence of at least two acute symptoms—such as 
headache, dizziness, nausea, or confusion—along with at least one 
clinical or laboratory finding supporting the diagnosis of brain 
injury are other criteria. Clinical findings may include abnor -
malities in neurologic examinations, while laboratory findings

could involve biomarkers indicative of brain injury. Neuroimag-
ing evidence of TBI, such as identifying clear trauma-related 
intracranial abnormalities on computed tomography (CT) scans 
or structural magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), also satisfies the 
diagnostic criteria. Importantly, these clinical signs, symptoms, 
and findings must not be fully explained by other confounding 
factors, ensuring that the observed symptoms are indeed due to 
the brain injury rather than other medical conditions (Fig. 44.3, 
Boxes 44.2 and 44.3).
556
The “mild” qualifier is excluded if any of the following severity 
indicators are present: loss of consciousness lasting more than 30 
minutes; a GCS score of less than 13 after 30 minutes postinjury; 
or posttraumatic amnesia extending beyond 24 hours. When 
neuroimaging is performed and reveals structural intracranial 
injury, the diagnosis can be specified as “mild TBI with neuroim-
aging evidence of structural intracranial injury.”
556 Conversely, if 
neuroimaging is normal, the diagnosis can be specified as “mild 
TBI without neuroimaging evidence of structural intracranial 
injury.”
556 In cases where neuroimaging is not conducted, no 
specific neuroimaging qualifier is applied. The term concussion is 
often used interchangeably with “mild TBI” in instances where 
neuroimaging is either normal or not clinically indicated.
556
A suspected mild TBI is considered when there is a biome -
chanically plausible mechanism of injury and one or more of the 
following conditions are met: the presence of at least two acute 
symptoms indicative of brain injury, even if the individual does 
not meet other criteria sufficient for a definitive diagnosis of mild 
TBI; at least two clinical examination or laboratory findings that 
suggest brain injury, though the individual does not meet the 
complete criteria for a confirmed diagnosis of mild TBI; or situa-
tions where it is unclear whether the clinical signs, acute symp -
toms, and available clinical or laboratory findings can be fully 
accounted for by confounding factors, thus creating uncertainty 
about the diagnosis.
556
Frequently, patients with concussion will report any number of 
associated symptoms, including memory loss, poor concentra -
tion, impaired emotional control, posttraumatic headaches 
(PTHs), sleep disorders, fatigue, irritability, dizziness, problems 
with visual acuity, depression, anxiety, personality changes, and 
seizures.
4,673 Problems resulting from these additional symptoms 
can impact ADLs, social relationships, and ability to perform in 
the workplace. For most individuals with mild TBI, symptoms 
resolve over time; however, a subset of patients will incur persis -
tent symptoms, classified as postconcussion syndrome, and may

nance imaging. A suspected mild TBI is indicated by dashed lines. (From Silverberg ND, Iverson GL, ACRM Brain 
Injury Special Interest Group Mild TBI Task Force members, et al. The American Congress of Rehabilitation 
Medicine diagnostic criteria for mild traumatic brain injury. Arch Phys Med Rehabil. 2023;104(8):1343–1355.)

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
American Congress of Rehabilitation Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Mild Traumatic Brain Injury556
Mild traumatic brain injury (TBI) is diagnosed when, following a biomechanically 
plausible mechanism of injury (Criterion 1) one or more of the criteria (i-iii) listed 
below are met.
 i.  One or more c linical signs (Criterion 2) attributable to brain injury.
 ii.  At least 2 acute symptoms (Criterion 3) and a t least one clinical or  
laboratory finding (Criterion 4) attributable to brain injury.
 iii.  Neuroima ging evidence of TBI, such as unambiguous trauma-related  
intracranial abnormalities on computed tomography or structural  
magnetic resonance imaging (Criterion 5).
Confounding factors do not fully account for the clinical signs (Criterion 2), 
acute symptoms (Criterion 3), and clinical examination and laboratory  
findings (Criterion 4) that are necessary for the diagnosis (Criterion 6).
Mild Qualifier: The “mild” qualifier is not used if any of the injury severity indica-
tors listed below are present. Instead, traumatic brain injury (TBI) is diagnosed 
(without the “mild” qualifier).
 i. Loss of consciousness dura tion greater than 30 minutes.
 ii. After 30 minutes,  a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) of less than 13.
 iii. P osttraumatic amnesia greater than 24 hours.
Neuroimaging Qualifier: If neuroimaging is abnormal (Criterion 5), the qualifier 
mild TBI “with neuroimaging evidence of structural intracranial injury” may be 
used. When neuroimaging is completed and found to be normal, the qualifier mild 
TBI “without neuroimaging evidence of structural intracranial injury” may be 
used. If neuroimaging is not completed, no qualifier is used.
Concussion: The diagnostic label “concussion” may be used interchangeably 
with “mild TBI” when neuroimaging is normal or not clinically indicated.
Suspected Mild TBI: A mild TBI is suspected when, following a biomechanically 
plausible mechanism of injury (Criterion 1), one or more of the 3 criteria listed 
below are met.
 i.  At least 2 acute symptoms (Criterion 3) and the person does not meet 
other criteria sufficient for diagnosing mild TBI.
 ii.  At least 2 c linical examination or laboratory findings (Criterion 4) but  
the person does not meet other criteria for diagnosing mild TBI.
 iii.  It is unc lear whether signs (Criterion 2), acute symptoms (Criterion 3), 
and available clinical or laboratory findings (Criterion 4) are accounted 
for by confounding factors (i.e., it is unclear if Criterion 6 is met).
See Box 44.3 for definitions and explanatory notes.
From Silverberg ND, Iverson GL, ACRM Brain Injury Special Interest Group Mild TBI Task Force  
members, et al. The American Congress of Rehabilitation Medicine diagnostic criteria for mild  
traumatic brain injury. Arch Phys Med Rehabil. 2023;104(8):1343–1355.
• BOX 44.2
American Congress of Rehabilitation Medicine Diagnostic Criteria for Mild Traumatic Brain Injury—
Definitions and Explanatory Notes556
Criterion 1: Mechanism of Injury
Traumatic brain injury (TBI) results from a transfer of mechanical energy to the 
brain from external forces resulting from the (1) head being struck with an object; 
(2) head striking a hard object or surface; (3) brain undergoing an acceleration/
deceleration movement without direct contact between the head and an object 
or surface; and/or (4) forces generated from a blast or explosion.
Notes: Criterion 1 can be met by direct observation (in person or video re -
view) or collateral (witness) report of the injury event, review of acute care re -
cords, or the person's recount of the injury event during an interview.
Criterion 2: Clinical Signs
The injury event causes an acute physiological disruption of brain function, as 
manifested by one or more of the clinical signs listed below.
 i .   Loss of consciousness immedia tely following injury (e.g., no protective 
action taken on falling after impact or lying motionless and unresponsive).
 ii.  Altera tion of mental status immediately following the injury (or upon  
regaining consciousness), evidenced by reduced responsiveness or  
inappropriate responses to external stimuli; slowness to respond to 
questions or instructions; agitated behavior; inability to follow two-part 
commands; or disorientation to time, place, or situation.
 iii.  Complete or partial amnesia for events immedia tely following the injury 
(or after regaining consciousness). If posttraumatic amnesia cannot be 
reliably assessed (e.g., due to polytrauma or sedating analgesics),  
retrograde amnesia (i.e., a gap in memory for events immediately  
preceding the injury) can be used as a replacement for this criterion.
 iv.  Other acute neurologic sign(s) (e.g.,  observed motor incoordination 
upon standing, seizure, or tonic posturing immediately following injury).
Notes: Criterion 2 can be met by direct observation (in person or video review), 
collateral (witness) report, review of acute care records, or, when none of these 
are available, the person's recount of the injury event.
Criterion 3: Acute Symptoms
The physiological disruption of brain function is manifested by 2 or more new or 
worsened symptoms from the list below.
 i.  Acute subjective altera tion in mental status: feeling confused, feeling 
disoriented, and/or feeling dazed.
 ii.  Physical symptoms:  headache, nausea, dizziness, balance problems,  
vision problems, sensitivity to light, and/or sensitivity to noise.
 iii.  Cognitive symptoms:  feeling slowed down, “mental fog,” difficulty  
concentrating, and/or memory problems.
 iv.  Emotional symptoms:  uncharacteristic emotional lability and/or irritability.
The symptoms may be from one or more categories (i.e., experiencing 2 symp -
toms within a single category is sufficient). Other symptoms may be present, but 
they should not be counted toward Criterion 3. The onset of acute subjective 
alteration in mental status occurs immediately following the impact or after re -
gaining consciousness. The onset of other symptoms (physical, cognitive, and 
emotional) may be delayed by a few hours, but they nearly always appear less 
than 72 hours from injury.
Notes: Criterion 3 can be met by (1) review of acute care documentation of the 
injured person's acute symptoms, (2) interviewing the injured person about the first 
few days following injury; (3) having the injured person complete a self-report  
rating scale documenting symptoms during the first few days following injury; or  
(4) collateral observation for an individual who cannot accurately report symptoms 
due to developmental stage (e.g., children under 5 years old) or preinjury disability.
Criterion 4: Clinical Examination and Laboratory Findings
The assessment findings listed below can also provide supportive evidence of 
brain injury.
 i. Cognitive impairment on acute c linical examination.
 ii. Balance impairment on acute c linical examination.
 iii.  Oculomotor impairment or symptom provoca tion in response to vestibu-
lar-oculomotor challenge on acute clinical examination.
 iv.  Eleva ted blood biomarker(s) indicative of intracranial injury.
Notes: Clinical and laboratory tests that meet standards of reliability and diag -
nostic accuracy should be considered for Criterion 4. Impairment in Criterion 
4i-iii is defined as a clinically meaningful discrepancy between postinjury test 
performance and age-appropriate normative reference data, or, where available, 
preinjury test performance. The diagnostic sensitivity of most clinical and labora-
tory tests decreases over the first 72 hours following injury and the rate of sen -
sitivity decline differs between specific tests.
Criterion 5: Neuroimaging
Trauma-related intracranial abnormalities on computed tomography or structural 
magnetic resonance imaging.
Notes: Neuroimaging is not necessary to diagnose mild TBI. Its primary 
clinical role is to rule out head and brain injuries that might require neurosurgical

require ongoing outpatient follow-up. The types of symptoms, the 
proportion of patients that have persistent symptoms, and the 
duration of symptoms required to define symptoms as “persis -
tent” are subject to debate. Reports of symptom duration to 
define persistent deficits can range between 3 and 12 months, 
with the most commonly utilized criteria for diagnosis of post -
concussion syndrome defined in the International Statistical Clas-
sification of Diseases and Health-Related Problems, T enth Revision 
(ICD-10).
673 The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental 
Disorders, Fifth Edition ( DSM-5) criteria for postconcussion  
syndrome has been revised substantially with the term mild  
neurocognitive impairment from TBI introduced instead of postcon-
cussion syndrome.4 Despite variability in the definitions and clas -
sification, some studies suggest that females are more likely to 
have postconcussion syndrome.
53,73,494,587
Athletes comprise a large proportion of the population that 
sustain mild TBI, and many of these are children. Special consid-
erations arise with this population regarding “on site” sports-
related concussion (SRC) screening tools, determining the most 
effective algorithm for return-to-play (RTP), use of safety equip -
ment, management and prevention of recurrent injuries, and 
academic performance. Athletes should be free of postconcussive 
symptoms before returning to competition. Recent evidence and 
expert consensus suggest a stepwise approach to return-to-school 
(RTS) guidelines for pediatric concussions.
473 The transition back 
to school after an SRC is crucial for children, adolescents, and 
young adults.
370 A systematic review found that 93% of athletes 
of all ages fully return to learning (RTL) without needing addi -
tional academic support within 10 days. 497 While many students 
resume their studies with minimal difficulty, those with high 
acute symptom severity or preexisting learning disabilities may 
face more challenges. T o minimize academic and social disrup-
tions, health care providers should avoid recommending complete 
rest and isolation, even in the first 24 to 48 hours. Instead, they 
should suggest a period of relative rest and encourage early return 
to daily activities, as long as symptoms increase only slightly and 
briefly (no more than 2 points on a 0–10 scale for less than an 
hour).
473 RTL strategies should be implemented as illustrated in 
Table 44.2.
The process of recovery and RTP after an SRC should follow a 
graduated stepwise rehabilitation approach. 473 Once postconcus-
sive symptoms have resolved, athletes should proceed to the next 
level once all criteria are met without a recurrence or worsening 
of concussion symptoms. In general, each step should take at least 
24 hours, so that athletes take a minimum of 1 week to proceed 
through the full RTP rehabilitation protocol. However, the time-
frame to RTP is not rigid and may vary with age and experience 
in the sport; therefore management should be individualized 
using the framework of a stepwise rehabilitation approach. For 
athletes who experience prolonged symptoms, each step may take 
longer than 24 hours, and if symptoms occur during the stepwise 
rehabilitation approach, the athlete should return to the previous 
asymptomatic level and attempt to progress again after being free 
from postconcussive symptoms at the lower level for another 
24-hour period (Table 44.3).
Same-season injuries often occur within 7 to 10 days of the 
initial injury, a timeframe in which there is increased vulnerability 
to damage with the second insult.
162,387 Notably, recent studies 
suggest that academic and cognitive exertion can impact postcon-
cussive symptoms, and cognitive testing performance should be 
considered when developing RTP protocols for athletes.
497 Several 
guidelines recommend the termination of participation in sports 
for the remainder of the season if an athlete sustains a third con-
cussion within that season.
1 Athletes who have one concussion are 
at increased risk for a subsequent concussion,238 therefore making 
a return to contact sports, even after a single concussion, a deci -
sion that requires careful consideration.
Acute Medical Management of Severe  
Traumatic Brain Injury
The care of individuals with TBI begins in the field. Evidence-
based guidelines put forth by the Brain T rauma Foundation and 
the American Association of Neurological Surgeons support a 
regionally organized trauma care system that addresses prehospital 
care and triage.361 The primary goal of prehospital care for trauma 
patients is to stabilize life-threatening conditions and prevent 
further injury, while also deciding the most appropriate hospital 
or other medical intervention in an acute care setting. When obtained, neuroim -
aging may reveal intracranial abnormalities indicative of TBI such as contusion(s) 
or intracranial hemorrhage.
Criterion 6: Not better accounted for by confounding factors
Confounding factors, including pre-existing and co-occurring health conditions, 
have been considered and determined to not fully account for the clinical signs, 
acute symptoms, and clinical examination and laboratory findings that are nec -
essary for the diagnosis.
Notes: A clinical sign only qualifies for Criterion 2 when it is not better ac -
counted for by acute musculoskeletal pain, psychological trauma, alcohol or 
substance intoxication, pulmonary or circulatory disruption, syncope prior to fall, 
or other confounding factors. Symptoms should only be counted toward Criterion 
3 when they are not better accounted for by drug, alcohol, or medication use; 
co-occurring physical injuries (e.g., musculoskeletal injury involving the neck or 
peripheral vestibular dysfunction) or psychological conditions (e.g., an acute 
stress reaction to trauma); pre-existing health conditions; or symptom exaggera-
tion. Criterion 4 findings must not be better accounted for by drug, alcohol, or 
medication use; co-occurring physical injuries or psychological conditions;  
pre-existing health conditions; or factors influencing the validity of the symptom 
reporting or test results.
General Notes: Consideration should be given to cultural and linguistic differ-
ences in symptom reporting and test performance. Caution is warranted when 
applying the diagnostic criteria for mild TBI to young children and individuals with 
preinjury cognitive and/or communication impairments. Due to developmental 
stage (e.g., children under 5 years old) or preinjury disability, an individual may 
not be able to accurately report symptoms in Criterion 3; thus, this criterion could 
be met based on proxy report or observation of related behaviors (e.g., changes 
in appetite or behaving out of character). An injured person's behavior should 
also be interpreted in the context of their developmental stage and preinjury 
functioning. Clinical and laboratory test interpretation requires age-appropriate 
scales and/or cut-off scores.
