973Chapter 44 Traumatic Brain Injury
for transfer. Research indicates that outcomes for general trauma 
patients improve when care is integrated within regional trauma 
systems, though the benefits of rapid stabilization versus direct 
transport to trauma centers remain a point of discussion. Studies 
have not consistently shown a definitive advantage of direct trans-
port to trauma centers for adult patients with TBI compared to 
initial treatment at nontrauma centers followed by transfer. How-
ever, some studies suggest potential benefits for direct transport, 
particularly in cases of severe TBI requiring timely neurosurgical 
intervention and in the pediatric population. Overall, the evi -
dence is mixed, with some studies indicating no significant differ-
ences in mortality or functional outcomes based on transport 
strategy, while others highlight the importance of minimizing 
delays in specialized care.361
ICP monitoring can reduce in-hospital and 2-week postinjury 
mortality for persons with severe TBI. 85 The current Brain 
T rauma Foundation guideline recommendations include moni -
toring ICP in all salvageable patients with severe TBI with a GCS 
score of 3 to 8 and abnormal CT scans, as well as in patients with 
normal CT scans who are over 40, exhibit motor posturing, or 
have a systolic blood pressure below 90 mm Hg. 85
CPP is the pressure gradient across the cerebral vascular bed, 
calculated as the difference between mean arterial pressure (MAP) 
and ICP . In severe TBI, elevated ICP affects this gradient, making 
CPP dependent on both MAP and ICP . Cerebral autoregulation 
maintains cerebral blood flow across various CPP levels through 
changes in vascular resistance. While CPP monitoring is part of 
TBI management, its direct impact on outcomes, independent of 
MAP and ICP , is uncertain. The optimal CPP value has been 
debated, with earlier recommendations suggesting high levels to 
restore autoregulation; however, more recent studies indicate that 
maintaining CPP above 70 mm Hg may increase the risk of acute 
respiratory distress syndrome. Current guidelines suggest target -
ing a CPP between 60 and 70 mm Hg, tailored to the patient's 
autoregulatory status, and caution against aggressive interventions 
to exceed 70 mm Hg due to associated risks. There is insufficient 
evidence for a Level I recommendation on this topic, but Level II 
B guidelines recommend CPP monitoring to reduce 2-week mor-
tality in patients with severe TBI.85
Following a TBI, both local and systemic factors contribute to 
ongoing brain damage, especially when oxygen or glucose delivery 
falls short, leading to cellular death. Advanced monitoring tech -
niques, such as transcranial Doppler (TCD), arterio-jugular 
venous oxygen difference (AVDO2), and local tissue oxygen mea-
surements, are used to assess cerebral blood flow and oxygenation. 
AVDO2 provides a global overview of cerebral oxygen extraction 
but may not accurately reflect conditions in both brain hemi -
spheres. Tissue monitors in the cerebral cortex measure local 
oxygen levels, though the dynamics between oxygen delivery and 
diffusion across the BBB are complex. Level III evidence suggests 
that jugular bulb monitoring of AVDO 2 might reduce mortality 
and improve outcomes at 3 and 6 months postinjury. Despite 
identifying poorer outcomes in patients with desaturations, Level 
II evidence did not show improved outcomes for patients who 
were monitored.85
Surgical treatment of severe TBI is indicated when intracere -
bral fluid collections or cerebral edema exert sufficient pressure to 
compress adjacent structures and/or cause herniation across dural 
or bony structures, threatening vital functions. Signs of elevated 
ICP include headache, papilledema, and nausea. Progression to 
herniation is accompanied by stupor and coma, pupillary changes, 
paresis, posturing, respiratory abnormalities, and eventually  
cardiopulmonary arrest. This mass effect can be appreciated on 
neuroimaging in the form of gross anatomic distortion, midline 
shift, or sulcal or ventricular effacement.199 Decompressive crani-
ectomy (DC), a surgical procedure that involves removing a por -
tion of the skull, is employed to alleviate this pressure and 
improve patient outcomes. However, the effectiveness of DC in 
managing severe TBI remains controversial due to a lack of com -
prehensive data from RCTs. Current guidelines indicate that 
bifrontal DC is not recommended for improving long-term out -
comes in patients with severe TBI and diffuse injuries. This is 
despite its ability to reduce ICP and shorten ICU stays. For better 
survival rates and improved neurologic outcomes, a larger crani -
ectomy is preferred over a smaller one. Ongoing research aims to 
provide more definitive evidence on the utility of DC, especially 
as a secondary intervention when medical therapies targeting ICP 
fail. These findings could significantly impact future recommen -
dations and clinical practices.85
Secondary complications occurring in the ICU can also nega -
tively affect TBI outcomes. Elevated glucose levels are associated 
with increased mortality after TBI, presumably due to increased 
lactic acid production leading to ischemia as well as impaired 
phosphorus metabolism. 522 Recent studies suggest that glucose 
variability, including low serum glucose levels, may adversely 
impact outcome. 383 Sodium imbalance can potentiate risks of 
seizure as well as worsen level of consciousness, as this electrolyte 
abnormality is directly related to volume status. Hyponatremia 
can be exacerbated by giving excessive volumes of intravenous 
fluids. This phenomenon occurs even in the setting of normal 
saline or Ringer lactate used for fluid resuscitation, making elec -
trolyte monitoring and volume status important in preventing 
secondary complications. Prevention and aggressive treatment of 
infections is also important, with multiple studies indicating that 
hospital-acquired pneumonia can have significant effects on long-
term outcome. 299,337 Hyperthermia also accelerates secondary 
insults to brain parenchyma, increasing ischemia375; thus mainte-
nance of normothermia is often a part of clinical critical care for 
those with severe injuries.33
Early initiation of nutrition is vitally important to the individ-
ual experiencing TBI. Metabolic rates can increase post-TBI, 
which have led to recommendations for caloric replacement above 
resting metabolic expenditure. However, more recent studies show 
this response to be attenuated because of modern neurocritical 
care, which more reliably includes sedation and paralytics.466 The 
gastrointestinal tract is preferred to maintain nutritional status. 
Though the literature does not yet support standard practice 
guidelines to optimize nutrition after msTBI, significant evidence 
exists suggesting that delayed nutrition replacement is associated 
with increased mortality.121,648
In the acute care management of patients with severe TBI, 
physical medicine and rehabilitation (PM&R) plays a critical role 
through specialized consultation services. By implementing a brain 
injury medicine (BIM) continuity consultation service, PM&R 
specialists can significantly enhance patient outcomes and health 
care efficiency. This model involves early and continuous involve-
ment of BIM-certified physiatrists who provide tailored recom -
mendations and interventions. Common interventions include 
managing sleep-wake cycles, neurostimulation, spasticity control, 
and agitation management. The service focuses on minimizing 
complications such as deep venous thrombosis (DVT) and infec -
tions and facilitates early mobilization and rehabilitation planning. 
Such proactive and specialized care can lead to reduced lengths of 
stay in acute care settings, fewer unplanned readmissions, and

improved recovery trajectories, particularly in patients with DoC. 
By coordinating care transitions and optimizing acute rehabilita -
tion processes, PM&R specialists contribute to improved func -
tional outcomes and cost savings in the management of severe 
brain injuries.654
Physiologic Measurements During Acute Care
SSEPs are recorded from the scalp after stimulation of a sensory 
or mixed nerve in the peripheral nervous system. SSEPs are often 
used in cases of severe brain injury to predict survival and out -
comes.268 In some cases, SSEPs are used as an adjunct to the 
clinical exam in determining brain death. 241 Hume and Cant 
found that bilateral loss of the median SSEP was indicative of 
impending death, and asymmetric findings observed in the corti -
cal response were indicative of severe residual deficits. 82 Their 
study and others indicate that serial SSEPs often change through 
the hospital course, and that the presence of bilateral cortical 
responses is a weak predictor of a good neurologic outcome. A 
meta-analysis of 41 studies reporting SSEPs in comatose patients 
and their outcomes found that they predicted nonawakening with 
a high level of certainty, especially so in individuals with hypoxic-
ischemic encephalopathy where less than 1% awakened if cortical 
responses were absent.514
EEG is routinely employed in the initial management of 
msTBI to monitor for electrographic seizures, and certain other 
features have been associated with depth of unconsciousness and 
both positive and negative clinical outcomes. 13 The presence of 
electrographic seizures or status epilepticus on continuous EEG 
(cEEG) acutely after TBI has been associated with increased 
likelihood of death or discharge to a skilled nursing facility, while 
the presence of sleep architecture is associated with a shorter acute 
hospitalization and earlier therapy participation. 532 Absence of  
the posterior dominant rhythm (PDR) is associated with depth of 
unconsciousness,13,339 and a retrospective study examining all 
patients with severe TBI who underwent cEEG within 30 days of 
injury found the presence of PDR predicted higher rates of in-
hospital survival, recovery of command following, and Glasgow 
Outcomes Scale - Extended (GOSE) score on discharge. 110
Pupillary reflex abnormalities in TBI can indicate complica -
tions associated with cerebral edema and herniation. They can be 
useful for lesion localization and in predicting mortality and 
global outcomes postinjury. The absence of the pupillary light 
response is very strongly associated with mortality and poor out -
comes after brain injury.60 Pupillary abnormalities have also been 
shown to herald increased ICP and delayed cortical ischemia in 
SAH.60 The International Mission on Prognosis and Analysis of 
Randomized Controlled T rials (IMPACT) in TBI has demon -
strated high predictability of post-TBI mortality and poor out -
come using a core model of age, injury severity (motor subscale of 
the GCS), pupillary reactivity, and midline shift.365
The body of work concerning fluid biomarkers for brain inju-
ries has expanded significantly over the past 5 years, and they 
show considerable potential as adjunctive tools for diagnosis and 
prognostication. The most studied markers are either (1) struc -
tural proteins or proteins contained in intracellular fluid or (2) 
markers of secondary injury and repair processes. Protein bio -
markers have been studied in the CSF and serum. CSF biomark -
ers have potential utility in that they can be viewed largely as a 
reflection of CNS pathology but can be nonspecific and are lim -
ited in that CSF is not routinely sampled in mild and moderate 
TBI. Blood biomarkers are much less invasive to obtain and are 
thought to appear after brain injury due to either microscopic or 
gross disruption of the BBB.10
Several assays promise to measure multiple markers in the 
clinically useful timescale and may permit certain groups of 
patients with mTBI to be managed without the radiation of CT 
imaging. Utilization of a calcium binding protein S100 subunit 
beta (S100 b) assay for mild TBI patients presenting to the ED 
permitted 20% to be discharged without CT in one study with 
no missed brain injuries on follow-up.19 Assays for blood levels of 
ubiquitin C-terminal hydrolase-L1 (UCH-L1) and glial fibrillary 
acidic protein (GFAP) were demonstrated to have both high sen-
sitivity and negative predictive value for intracranial pathology on 
CT44,142 and are now available in a point of care whole blood test 
that provides accurate results in 15 minutes. 321 Fluid biomarker 
utility is less clear in the moderate and severe brain injury popula-
tion due to complexity of care being rendered, confounding 
variables, and established clinical decision-making patterns that 
this additional information may not influence meaningfully.10
Neuroimaging for Clinical Management
Neuroimaging plays a critical role in the management of brain 
injuries for initial evaluation, monitoring of recovery and compli-
cations, and prognostication. While CT and MRI are both well-
established modalities, more recently developed imaging methods 
with demonstrated clinical utility illustrate nerve tracts, cerebral 
tissue perfusion, and metabolic activity and are discussed in the 
section “Neuroimaging Modalities in T raumatic Brain Injury 
Research.” Mass lesions appreciable on CT (Fig. 44.4) are usually 
comprised of blood but can also result from cerebral swelling or air 
accumulation. EDH lie between the dura and the cranium, are 
biconvex in appearance, and typically have an arterial origin. SDH 
occupy the potential space between the dura and arachnoid mem-
brane and can track along much of the cerebral hemisphere, giving 
them a characteristic crescent appearance. SAH lie under the 
arachnoid membrane and can be seen deep in the cortical gyri and 
cisterns. Cerebral contusions and intraparenchymal hematomas 
(IPH) are both lesions comprised of traumatized brain tissue and 
blood and are arbitrarily delineated by approximately what pro -
portion of each is present (Fig. 44.5). DAI can be more subtle on 
CT unless severe but are more readily imaged on MR sequences 
(Fig. 44.6).6 While it is expected for air to be present in the cra -
nium after various surgical procedures, a tension pneumocephalus 
is a potential surgical emergency where pressure is sufficient to 
compress and separate the frontal lobes, whose sloped appearance 
on axial imaging is classically compared to that of Mt. Fuji.420
Noncontrast CT is the modality of choice for the initial evalu-
ation of individuals suspected of experiencing msTBI. 550 CT is 
advantageous as it allows for rapid imaging that accurately detects 
facial and skull fractures, as well as mass lesions requiring immedi-
ate surgical attention. Serial CT scans may be used to evaluate 
recovery or to monitor for additional complications. For example, 
if an individual does not progress as expected or declines, repeat 
CT scans can assess for problems such as new or evolving bleeding 
or hydrocephalus.
Less than 10% of mild TBI cases have positive CT findings, 
and less than 1% require neurosurgical intervention. 280 Multiple 
decision-making tools are available to assist with determining 
whether imaging is indicated,156 including the New Orleans Cri -
teria (NOC), 253 the Canadian CT Head Rule (CCHR), 567 the 
National Emergency X-Ray Utilization Study (NEXUS-II), 436 
and the Pediatric Emergency Care Applied Research Network

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
(PECARN).31 These tools provide additional clinical and circum-
stantial criteria, which when present increase the likelihood of a 
positive CT finding, including advanced age, hypocoagulability, 
signs of basilar skull fracture, seizures, vomiting, headaches, and 
significant PTA. The nature of the mechanism of injury can also 
increase the likelihood of a positive CT finding.
MRI utilizes strong magnetic fields to excite protons in water-
containing tissues and records the radio waves they emit. Tissues 
with different characteristics emit variable signals, permitting the 
construction of a high-resolution image. MRI sequences take 
longer to acquire than CT , making them more susceptible to 
motion artifact and less ideal in unstable patients. Moreover, the 
strong magnetic field in the immediate vicinity of the scanner 
poses significant logistic obstacles in medically complicated indi -
viduals who are often tethered to machinery or may have retained 
ferromagnetic foreign bodies or implanted devices. The capacity 
for higher resolution and more varied sequencing makes MRI the 
preferred modality in the evaluation and prognostication of 
lesions in small structures such as the brainstem and for DAI.
651 
Specific MRI sequences are especially useful in the evaluation of 
TBI. While T1-weighted imaging is preferable to define anatomy, 
T2-weighted sequences highlight edematous tissue with higher 
water content. Fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR)  
suppresses free fluid, improving visualization of periventricular 
lesions, cortical lesions, and nonhemorrhagic shear injuries.  
Gradient, gradient echo, and susceptibility weighted imaging are 
particularly sensitive for blood breakdown products associated 
with DAI.
440
Evaluation and Management of Disorders of 
Consciousness in TBI
Most patients with significant TBIs experience initial loss of con-
sciousness, with some persisting as a DoC. A large review of TBI 
model systems data collected over three decades found that 74% 
of more than 17,000 patients demonstrated some degree of 
decreased arousal, with 12% of that group remaining in a DoC 
until transition to a rehabilitation setting, where 82% regained 
consciousness.
324 The three goals of managing patients with DoC 
are to (1) accurately diagnose the degree of DoC; (2) increase the 
level of consciousness; and (3) prevent or manage complications 
associated with severe TBI and general immobility.
215
Four primary DoCs are recognized: (1) coma; (2) coma, unre-
sponsive wakefulness syndrome (UWS, or vegetative state); (3) 
minimally conscious state (MCS 1/2); and (4) posttraumatic 
confusional state. Cognitive motor dissociation is recognized as 
evidence of at least partial awareness despite clinical presentation 
as a DoC and has been documented with the development of 
functional imaging modalities such as functional MRI (fMRI) 
and EEG. Additionally, locked-in syndrome is a state of profound 
motor impairment resulting most commonly from pontine  
or midbrain insult that is not a DoC but can present as one  
(Table 44.4).
214
Assessing Level of Disorder of Consciousness
It is recommended that DoC be assessed clinically with standard-
ized behavioral instruments to minimize interrater variability and 
multiple biases.
215 Accurate and reliable assessment of level of 
consciousness is vitally important because it plays a key role in 
high stakes clinical decision making, including withdrawal or 
care. It has been demonstrated that up to 40% of assessments of 
patients who were at least minimally conscious were inaccurate in 
the direction of a lower level of consciousness.
222,542 The JFK 
Coma Recovery Scale Revised (CRS-R) is the most used validated 
instrument for the assessment of DoC. The CRS-R has six sub -
scales representing different neurologic domains and 23 total 
points, with a score of 10 or higher denoting MCS. It is impor-
tant to be mindful that multiple factors may influence assessment 
of DoC, including medical comorbidities, environmental factors, 
superimposed focal neurologic deficits such as aphasia or visual 
impairment, misrecognition of reflexive or purposeful behaviors, 
and over- or under-utilization of family input.
56
Responsiveness and awareness can also be assessed with 
advanced diagnostic modalities that are less commonly available. 
EEG is noninvasive, portable, and relatively available and has been 
shown in various paradigms to have potential to differentiate UWS 
from MCS and serve as a foundation for the development of  
communication systems with patients with covert consciousness. 
Positron emission tomography (PET) measures glucose uptake 
and has been shown to differentiate UWS, MCS-, and MCS1 
based on differential cerebral metabolism. Advanced MRI modali-
ties such as diffusion tensor imaging can illustrate disrupted path-
ways, while fMRI can also show reactivity to passive stimulation 
and the overall health of the reticular activating system.
536
Interventions for Disorders of Consciousness
Sensory stimulation programs form the cornerstone of many 
DoC programs due to their noninvasiveness, ease of implemen -
tation, and relative cost compared to other interventions. They 
can utilize motor activity, visual stimuli, music therapy or famil -
iar voices, or integrate multiple senses simultaneously.
595 The 
Familiar Auditory Stimulation T raining protocol (FAST) makes 
use of brief autobiographical stories told by acquaintances of the 
patient and played as recordings at regular intervals, with one 
study showing improvement in CRS-R scores over time and 
increased fMRI activation compared to control, although 
improvements did not amount to progression from one level of 
DoC to another.
471

Neuromodulatory interventions, invasive and noninvasive, 
are being developed to directly improve arousal or prime patients 
to respond to other treatments. Noninvasive modalities include 
transcranial direct current stimulation, repeated transcranial 
magnetic stimulation, transcutaneous auricular vagal nerve stim -
ulation, and low-intensity focused ultrasound pulse, and invasive 
interventions include deep brain stimulation and vagus nerve 
stimulation. Of these interventions, transcranial direct current 
stimulation has been shown in multiple RCTs to marginally 
improve some patients with DoC on behavioral assessments.
595 
Pharmacologic interventions can be used to promote arousal and 
behavioral persistence.
A wide variety of pharmacologic interventions are employed in 
patients with DoC with varying levels of evidence for efficacy and 
significant practice variability. These agents typically have dopa -
minergic, noradrenergic, or cholinergic properties, such as aman-
tadine, bromocriptine, carbidopa-levodopa, methylphenidate, 
atomoxetine, modafinil, and donepezil.
374 Amantadine is a dopa-
mine agonist and an N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) antagonist 
that was shown in a large RCT to improve patients with pro -
longed DoC more quickly than those who received placebo.
216 As 
no other medication has supporting evidence that approaches this 
level, multiple guidelines recommend amantadine for patients in 
traumatic DoC.
215 Zolpidem is a nonbenzodiazepine sedative 
hypnotic with activity on gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) A 
receptors and has been shown in a minority of patients with DoC 
to improve arousal, at times dramatically. This paradoxical effect 
is hypothesized to be through thalamic disinhibition mediated by 
activity on the globus pallidus interna.
540 A retrospective study of 
95 zolpidem trials at one center’s DoC program found 11.6% to 
be “responders” and recommended all patients with DoC receive 
zolpidem trials.
693
Evaluation of Posttraumatic Amnesia
As an individual emerges from coma and can respond to verbal 
inquiry, instruments such as the Galveston Orientation and 
Amnesia T est (GOAT)
349 or the Orientation Log (O-Log) 276 are 
used to track progress through the PTA phase of recovery. Both 
instruments measure and score serial responses to orientation 
questions and allow for the objective scoring of the responses 
elicited from the individual. Emergence from PTA is then defined 
by the consistent attainment of a score of 75 on the GOAT or a 
25 on the O-Log.
While rehabilitation scales, such as the GOAT and O-Log, 
generally focus on disorientation and amnestic symptoms, other 
elements of confusion (i.e., delirium and psychiatric symptoms 
including agitation) can occur during the acute phase of TBI 
recovery. There is increased awareness of the need to measure 
these symptoms and to develop instruments that bridge gaps 
between scales traditionally used to measure PTA and those  
traditionally used to measure delirium.
380,586 One example is the 
Neurobehavioral Rating Scale, 347 which combines portions of  
the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale469 with additional items to mea-
sure other psychiatric symptoms associated with TBI. Other tools 
with more narrow uses include the Agitated Behavior Scale and 
the Apathy Evaluation Scale.
87
The Ranchos Levels of Cognitive Functioning Scale Revised 
(RLAS-R)356 is a widely accepted tool used to describe the process 
of cognitive recovery as an individual emerges from DoC, then 
progresses toward emergence from PTA/delirium, then to near 
normal cognitive functioning ( Table 44.5). The original scale

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Inpatient Rehabilitation Assessment and 
Management
The focus of inpatient rehabilitation (IPR) following TBI is to 
assist each patient in improving functional independence. Ideally, 
with good recovery, individuals will return home and succeed in 
eventual community reentry. The basic rehabilitation team is 
comprised of a multidisciplinary group of specialists including a 
physiatrist, physical therapist(s) (PT), occupational therapist(s) 
(OT), speech language pathologist(s) (SLP), as well as other hos -
pital staff including nursing, social work, and case management 
staff. The cognitive and behavioral issues associated with TBI may 
pose unique challenges to the provision of rehabilitation treat -
ment. Ideally, team members are trained to work with patients 
who have sustained TBI. Neuropsychologists are also often 
included as key members of the team.
Role of the Neuropsychologist in Inpatient 
Rehabilitation
The role of the neuropsychologist in the IPR service is multifac -
eted. The neuropsychologist provides ongoing assessment of 
recovery of cognitive function and works with therapy and nurs -
ing staff to integrate information about cognitive abilities and 
limitations into implementation of effective and appropriate 
rehabilitation goals for the patient. Ideally, the neuropsychologist 
is available to observe and cotreat with other therapists as needed. 
Neuropsychologists have specialized training in brain-behavior 
relationships and expertise in the testing and evaluation of cogni-
tion; they also apply that expertise to the evaluation of interac -
tions between cognitive strengths/limitations and behavior and 
often assist with organization and implementation of individual 
behavior plans. In some settings, psychologists function both as 
neuropsychologists and rehabilitation psychologists. Rehabilita -
tion psychology focuses on adjustment to disability and other 
personal, social, and situational issues in an effort to assist indi -
viduals with all types of disabilities to work toward and maintain 
a healthy, satisfying lifestyle.
Medical Rehabilitation Evaluation, 
Complications, and Management
During the initial evaluation on the IPR unit, a physiatrist or 
rehabilitation specialist will conduct a thorough neurologic exam-
ination. This exam often includes traditional physical examina -
tion components described elsewhere in this textbook. Often, 
depending on the individual’s medical status, a complete evalua-
tion cannot be conducted at first attempt and may need to be 
completed later. As awareness and communication improve, it is 
not uncommon to discover additional injuries that were previ -
ously missed due to the individual’s inability to express pain or 
participate in assessment. Physical exam measures include the 
description of motor weakness, abnormal muscle tone, and pres -
ence or absence of contractures. For those individuals with a 
decreased level of consciousness and cognitive disorders, it is vital 
to perform serial measures to document their level of awareness. 
The CRS-R can be utilized to evaluate DoC with some caution.17 
Also included in the evaluation should be documentation of 
brainstem reflexes such as pupillary reflexes, “doll’s eyes,” and the 
response to caloric stimulation. While some degree of medical 
stability has been achieved by the time an individual has been 
transferred from the ICU to IPR, medical complications can and 
do occur while the individual is on the rehabilitation unit. The 
next sections describe some medical issues and complications of 
particular concern for individuals with a recent history of TBI. 
Assessment and feedback from members of the rehabilitation 
therapy team often provide information vital to the diagnosis of 
these medical issues.
Posttraumatic Seizures
Posttraumatic seizures (PTS) are a significant complication aris -
ing from TBI. PTS commonly has been defined as occurring in 
the immediate period ( ,24 hours after injury), early period  
(24 hours to 7 days after injury), or late period ( .7 days after 
injury).511 Posttraumatic epilepsy (PTE) is late PTS and can also 
be defined as a single seizure occurring more than 7 days after an 
injury.511 The International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) 
characterizes epilepsy as either two seizures at least 24 hours apart 
or a single seizure with a high likelihood of recurrence. 191 Given 
the increased risk of seizure recurrence following a TBI, a single 
late PTS meets the ILAE's criteria for PTE. PTS accounts for 
20% of symptomatic seizures and 5% of all seizures in the gen -
eral population.163 Up to 86% of patients with one seizure after 
TBI will have a second within 2 years of their injury. 246 More-
over, PTE is associated with increased disability and limitations 
with ADLs, driving, participation, and life satisfaction. 26,78 
Recent prognostic models have identified factors including surgi-
cal evacuation procedures, SDH, cerebral contusion load, injury 
severity, depressed skull fracture, psychiatric history, seizure dur -
ing acute hospitalization, and preinjury history of incarceration 
as increasing risk for PTE. 29,512
The neurobiology of PTE is not well understood. However, 
neuroinhibitory molecules like adenosine and GABA afford rela -
tive protection from this complication. In fact, genetic variability 
within the ubiquitous adenosine A1 receptor and the adenosine 
regulatory cycle have been linked with risk for PTS occurring 
within the first week and also the development of PTE. 140,633 
Further, genetic variation in the glutamic acid decarboxylase 
(GAD1) gene also contributes genetic risk to PTE develop -
ment.133 Conversely, glutamate-mediated excitotoxicity and 
genetic variation within glutamate transporters 334,510 are associ -
ated with the development of PTE. One report suggests that acute 
inflammatory burden for IL-1 b and genetic variation within the 
gene coding for IL-1 b may accelerate both epileptogenesis and 
the development of PTE.139
Phenytoin is commonly used for early prophylaxis against PTS 
development and treatment. The drug acts by diminishing excit -
atory tone and augmenting inhibitory neurotransmitter systems in 
cortical and subcortical structures. Previous clinical trials suggest 
that phenytoin therapy for 1 week provides protection against 
early PTS.594 Notably, published treatment guidelines for phenyt-
oin are now over 10 years old and are based on recommendations 
derived from a (seminal) clinical trial published over 20 years ago 
showing that patients with severe TBI should receive prophylactic 
treatment with phenytoin for 7 days after injury. 594 The Associa-
tion of American Neurologists still recommends early intervention 
with intravenous phenytoin given as a loading dose as soon as 
possible followed by a 7-day course in the moderate-to-severely 
brain-injured population.91 Additionally, phenytoin use beyond 
the first week postinjury was not supported,594 and both clinical143 
and experimental 132 studies suggest adverse effects on recovery 
with prolonged phenytoin use.
Studies indicate that newer drugs, including the synaptic vesi -
cle protein 2A (SV2A) modulator levetiracetam (Keppra), are

safe, effective, do not require active monitoring of drug levels, and 
are growing in use clinically for PTS prevention. 700 However, 
research specifically examining their efficacy in preventing PTS 
following severe TBI is limited, despite an increasing trend in 
their use for this purpose. Recent small clinical reports suggest 
levetiracetam use for seizure prophylaxis over phenytoin.
331 In 
2010, a prospective randomized, single-blinded study involving 
52 patients compared intravenous levetiracetam to intravenous 
phenytoin in patients with severe TBI.
301 The study found that 
patients treated with levetiracetam had better long-term out-
comes, as measured by the Disability Rating Scale (DRS) and the 
Glasgow Outcome Scale (GOS), than those treated with phenyt -
oin. There were no significant differences between the groups in 
terms of seizure occurrence during continuous EEG monitoring 
(conducted in the first 72 hours) or at 6 months. Mortality rates 
and side effects were similar between the groups, except that 
levetiracetam-treated patients experienced fewer instances of 
worsened neurologic status and gastrointestinal issues. Khan and 
colleagues
301 conducted a meta-analysis comparing levetiracetam 
and phenytoin for PTS prophylaxis in TBI, including 1,186 
patients. The analysis found no significant difference in the inci -
dence of PTS between the two groups (levetiracetam 5.4% vs. 
phenytoin 3.4%, p 5 0.96).
301 Given the promising use of leve -
tiracetam for seizure prophylaxis in patients with severe TBI and 
its favorable side effect profile, it is increasingly considered a viable 
option for managing PTE and for early seizure prevention; how -
ever, additional studies are needed to confirm this finding.
577
In clinical practice, PTE is treated similarly to other symptom-
atic epilepsies, with antiseizure medications (ASMs) being the  
primary treatment. However, specific data on the effectiveness of 
certain ASMs or combinations for PTE are limited. Newer ASMs, 
like levetiracetam, may offer fewer interactions and lower metabolic 
toxicity, along with better cognitive side effect profiles.
247,697 
Pharmaco-resistance, the failure of two or more ASMs to control 
seizures, is a significant issue in PTE, with younger onset age, focal 
or mixed seizure types, status epilepticus, and interictal epileptiform 
discharges being predictors.
686 Despite high pharmaco-resistance 
rates, epilepsy surgery evaluations are infrequent in PTE popula -
tions,264 likely due to concerns about multifocality and patho -
physiologic variability.242 A meta-analysis showed slightly higher 
rates of unfavorable outcomes after temporal lobe surgery in PTE 
compared to nontraumatic epilepsy,
186 though the difference was 
not clinically significant, and the overall seizure freedom rate 
(70.1%) was comparable.
661 Similar excellent outcomes were 
reported for patients with extratemporal lobe PTE undergoing 
intracranial EEG evaluations.
242
Despite recommendations supporting limited treatment for 
seizure prophylaxis, 593 people who have sustained TBI often 
receive PTS prophylaxis on a long-term basis with anticonvulsant 
medications that require regular monitoring and are often associ -
ated with unwanted side effects, including sedation.
226 Individu-
als who go on to develop PTE also experience similar issues with 
long-term treatment.
143,561 Recent evidence using an experimental 
model of TBI demonstrates that long-term treatment with phe -
nytoin after injury leads to increased cell death and poorer func -
tion on cognitive behavioral tasks. 132 This work further supports 
following current guidelines for PTS prophylaxis with phenytoin, 
and prolonged therapy should be minimized when possible. 
Experimental work, however, suggests that daily treatment with 
levetiracetam for 3 weeks postinjury has multiple neuroprotective 
effects and improves multiple neurobehavioral outcomes in a rat 
model of TBI,
699 a finding that is consistent with neuroprotection 
characteristics of this drug in other studies. 249,646
Heterotopic Ossification
Heterotopic ossification (HO) (Fig. 44.7) is a common complica-
tion after TBI and is a poorly understood process by which ectopic 
bone is formed outside of the skeleton. Incidence of HO after 
severe TBI ranges from 11% t o 28%.
256 Approximately 10% of 
patients with HO following a closed head injury experience  
clinically significant joint immobility.414 Hemiplegic, spastic, trau-
matized, and immobilized joints tend to be more at risk, and  
HO most commonly involves the hips and elbows, followed  
by the shoulders, then knees. Incidence may be higher in those

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
sustaining military-related blast injuries, where TBI and trau-
matic amputation—both of which increase the risk of HO  
development—often cooccur. 196,490 Individuals with more severe 
TBI as well as associated immobility, spasticity, and fractures seem to 
be at relatively greater risk for this complication. Dysautonomia is 
also linked with higher HO risk.256 Among individuals undergoing 
acute IPR for TBI, HO is considered a risk factor for poorer out-
comes and decreased home discharge rates.281 Blood markers associ-
ated with bone metabolism (i.e., alkaline phosphatase, creatine 
phosphokinase, osteocalcin) are nonspecific for HO.444 X-rays can 
identify HO in more advanced cases. However, bone scans are more 
sensitive in identifying early and asymptomatic cases.
Although HO pathophysiology after TBI is poorly under -
stood, evidence suggests that CNS processes facilitate HO bone 
formation. Bone remodeling is subject to central control through 
the sympathetic nervous system, which is partially regulated by 
the endocannabinoid system and affects systemic inflammation. 
After experimental TBI, cannabinoid mediated down-regulation 
of norepinephrine at osteoblasts contributes to increased bone 
formation.581 Other research suggests that humoral factors in 
human CSF after TBI are osteoinductive; however, specific fac -
tors have not been identified. 208 Leptin, a metabolic protein, is 
also a potential mediator of HO through both central and 
peripheral mechanisms. 94 Other biochemical mediators impli -
cated with HO include transforming growth factor b, bone 
morphogenetic protein (BMP), and vascular endothelial growth 
factor.272
Common prophylactic methods include antiinflammatory 
medications like indomethacin, along with irradiation and bisphos-
phonates. Nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are 
effective in preventing HO formation in total joint replacement 
recipients,450 and mounting evidence suggests that NSAIDs are a 
useful preventative in spinal cord injury (SCI),28 but less is known 
about the specific effects of NSAIDs on HO after TBI. At this 
time, two systematic reviews have failed to provide adequate 
research evidence to support these therapies for acute HO treat -
ment.28,250 Once HO formation occurs, it can significantly affect 
joint range of motion and mobility. HO formation takes several 
months to mature. After maturation is complete, irradiation and/
or excision of the ectopic bone can often improve joint motion and 
mobility,430 and studies suggest that surgical excision is the most 
effective therapy for HO after TBI.28 After the initial surgery, 20% 
of patients experience recurrent symptoms of HO, including a 
reduction in ROM, and 6% of these patients require a subsequent 
resection of HO.15
Deep Venous Thrombosis
DVT is a significant source of morbidity and mortality risk for 
patients with TBI. In the severe TBI population, pulmonary 
embolus secondary to DVT is a significant cause of death, and the 
estimated incidence of DVT is 40%. 105 Although there are no 
guidelines for routine screening for DVT upon IPR admission, 
individuals diagnosed with DVT during the rehabilitation stay are 
more prone to acute hospital transfers and longer lengths of stay 
regardless of admission functional levels. 484 Increasing evidence 
supports the safe use of either heparin or low-molecular-weight 
heparin within 24 to 72 hours after severe TBI or intracranial 
bleed.178,209 Early pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis in urgent neu-
rosurgical patients with TBI may lower thromboembolism risk, 
though the evidence is not conclusive, 503 and early prophylaxis 
may increase the likelihood of repeated surgery, especially within 
the first 3 days postprocedure.81 These decisions often are made in 
consultation with the neurosurgeon about the risk of rebleed after 
review of an individual’s neuroimaging studies.
Individuals at highest risk for DVT include those of advanced 
age, severe injury, prolonged immobilization, significant fractures, 
and presence of clotting disorder. 515 Unfortunately, these same 
individuals may also share certain characteristics that would 
increase the risk of bleeding if prescribed DVT prophylaxis, 
including a higher fall risk, agitation, or unsafe living conditions. 
In those individuals who cannot undergo pharmacologic prophy-
laxis for DVT due to risk of bleeding, mechanical compression 
devices are used. Despite limited study, mechanical compression 
devices are thought to be less effective in clinical practice than in 
controlled trials. It is important that the device is consistently 
used daily and that it is appropriately sized. For individuals with 
agitation or requiring restraints, tolerance of these devices may be 
low. There is no clear definition for length of DVT prophylaxis in 
the TBI population; however, daily ambulation over increasing 
distances reduces risk.
Swallowing/Nutrition
msTBI is associated with specific nutritional needs. After TBI, 
individuals demonstrate increased caloric requirements due to 
hypermetabolism, increased energy expenditure, and increased 
protein loss. Early initiation of enteral nutritional support may 
decrease morbidity and mortality, shorten hospital length of stay, 
and potentially improve immune function. 66,344 Parenteral sup -
port during acute care for those still showing a negative energy 
balance with enteral feeding may be beneficial. 344 Nutritional 
status monitoring can include review of laboratories and weights 
and, in more complex cases, metabolic cart studies. Clinical dieti-
cians are important team members in this regard. Caloric supple-
mentation is not the only focus of nutritional support. Data 
support the role of nutraceutical supplementation (i.e., probiot -
ics) to decrease risk of infection as well as vitamin D, omega-3, 
and zinc supplementation for their potential benefits. Specific 
amounts and formulations need to be further clarified through 
specific studies.128,546
Factors that lead to the decision for a more permanent form of 
enteral nutrition include whether nasogastric nutrition can be 
consistently maintained, if the location of the tube is an agitating 
factor, and potential length of time nonoral nutrition is needed. 
Once a permanent tube is placed, it is expected to be in place for 
at least 4 to 6 weeks to decrease risk of complications associated 
with removal. The specific route of long-term nutritional support 
(i.e., gastrostomy vs. jejunostomy) is not uniform. Decision  
making as to whether to place a tube directly in the stomach ver -
sus more distally in the jejunum is based on presence of gastro -
esophageal reflux. Among individuals requiring nonoral nutrition 
when they go home, the gastric tube allows for bolus feeding, 
which better approximates natural feeding especially when sched-
uled near meal times.
As the individual’s level of alertness improves, swallowing evalu-
ation can include a bedside swallowing assessment or video fluo -
roscopy. The Yale Swallow Protocol (YSP)653 is an effective bedside 
screening tool for dysphagia in patients with msTBI in acute care 
settings with a high sensitivity and negative predictive value. Stud-
ies have shown an 88.2% positive predictive value, with a 93.8% 
negative predictive value, indicating a low risk of aspiration for 
those who pass the protocol. Despite some false positives, the YSP’s 
reliability and high interrater agreement make it a valuable method 
for early intervention and preventing complications in this patient 
population.653 Because normal gag reflexes and good cough reflexes

are not completely predictive of normal swallow function, video 
fluoroscopy (otherwise known as a modified barium swallow study 
[MBSS]) often identifies silent aspiration and improves clinical 
decision making when relaxing dietary restrictions. 666 An MBSS 
tracks barium-laced food particles through the oral, pharyngeal, 
and upper esophageal stages of swallowing performed when sitting 
upright. The MBSS allows for screening of the esophagus to the 
lower esophageal sphincter during swallowing; it does require 
some exposure to radiation and the need for the patient to be 
upright and be able to be transported to a radiation suite. FEES is 
an endonasal fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing that 
allows for a direct visualization of the pharyngeal phase of swallow-
ing. FEES provides visualization of the pharyngeal stage of swal -
lowing from the superior view, while the MBSS provides a lateral 
visualization of the oral, pharyngeal, and cervical esophageal stages 
of swallowing. FEES provides secondary assessment of velopharyn-
geal closure and/or laryngeal/pharyngeal surfaces and functions. 
However, it can be challenging to perform in the setting of cranio-
facial trauma. The decision on which instrumental procedure 
(MBSS vs. FEES) to use to evaluate for dysphagia is dependent on 
provider preference and on patient condition, with no procedure 
clearly superior to the other. Even after evaluation, the institution 
of oral nutrition is often done in a step-wise manner, evaluating 
the effects of behavior on feeding, as well as variations in level of 
arousal and fatigue during the day.
Bowel and Bladder Dysfunction
Loss of bladder and bowel control is common after TBI. Injury to 
cortical and subcortical structures can lead to loss of control over 
these functions or dyscoordination of sphincter management. 
Approximately 62% of individuals who have sustained significant 
TBI experience early incidence of urinary incontinence. The pat -
terns of dysfunction include an uninhibited overactive bladder, as 
well as poor perception of bladder fullness and poor sphincter 
control. During acute care and rehabilitation, urinary tract infec -
tions are common, impacting functional outcome on discharge, 
skin integrity, and increasing risk of discharge to long-term 
care.104 T reatment options include behavioral interventions such 
as timed voiding. Caution must be used in initiating anticholin -
ergic medications due to their adverse cognitive effects.
Bowel dysfunction after TBI includes incontinence and consti-
pation. Constipation is common due to impaired physical mobil-
ity, use of constipating medications, and dietary influences. Bowel 
programs include stool softeners, stimulant suppositories, and 
hydration. Bowel incontinence is often associated with more 
severe injury and poorer functional status. It may also be due to 
diarrhea, frequently seen with the use of enteral feeds or from 
infectious causes.197
Airway/Pulmonary Management
Pulmonary complications after TBI directly relate to the effects of 
trauma, including pneumothorax, hemothorax, flail chest, and rib 
fractures. Neurologic level of injury may lead to respiratory fail -
ure, pulmonary edema, and airway complications. Pneumonia is 
the most common complication observed in acute care and reha -
bilitation, occurring in 60% of individuals, 662 and is associated 
with poor long-term outcomes. 299,337 The presence of respiratory 
failure and need for tracheostomy are strongly related to longer 
acute care and rehabilitation length of stay. They are also associ -
ated with lower functional status on the DRS and the Functional 
Independence Measure 1 year after injury. 164 However, early  
tracheostomy ( #8 days) is associated with shorter mechanical 
ventilation times and shorter hospital stay but not hospital mor -
tality. The results of recent studies indicate that performing an 
early tracheostomy in patients with severe TBI reduces the risk of 
secondary complications and hospital-acquired adverse events, 
thereby enhancing the chances for earlier rehabilitation and dis -
charge.52,588
Many individuals who need a tracheostomy in acute care 
regain sufficient pulmonary and neurologic function later to 
undergo decannulation. Decannulation is considered when indi -
viduals no longer require ventilation, can manage their secretions, 
and are at low risk for aspiration. Usually, decannulation is 
achieved by serial tracheostomy changes decreasing the cannula 
diameter and then by capping the tube. Prior to capping the tra -
cheostomy, a speaking valve allows phonation through the open 
tracheostomy. Phonation is tolerated with a smaller diameter tra-
cheostomy, and the cuff on the tracheostomy must be deflated for 
safety. Generally, trained staff place the valve after they have veri-
fied cuff deflation. Valve tolerance is measured by the ability to 
maintain oxygen saturations and speak clear, long sentences 
without signs of breathlessness. T racheostomy removal occurs 
once individuals tolerate these steps. Visualization of the airway 
prior to decannulation via laryngoscopy can assess for tracheal 
stenosis, subglottic stenosis, glottic stenosis, and tracheal granu -
loma.457 These tracheal abnormalities are associated with a high 
risk of respiratory difficulties requiring reintubation and possible 
surgical intervention.
Decision making in decannulation of patients at Rancho Levels 
II and III is more difficult. Poor pulmonary toilet and variable 
central respiratory status in this group leads to pneumonia and 
sepsis and increased morbidity and mortality with decannulation. 
Predictors of successful decannulation include history of pneumo-
nia, increased cognitive status, and adequate swallowing and 
cough reflexes.166 In these individuals, clinicians should consider 
whether decannulation will make care easier or more difficult in 
the next level of care for the individual. Timing of decannulation 
is also important because environmental factors (i.e., weather) can 
lead to respiratory illness.
Spasticity and Contractures
Spasticity is a common problem observed with upper motor neu-
ron damage that commonly occurs with TBI. Spasticity is clini -
cally defined as a velocity-dependent increase in tonic stretch 
reflexes with exaggerated tendon jerk responses.341 Other compo-
nents of the upper motor syndrome, such as loss of autonomic 
control, decreased dexterity, and limb weakness, often occur in 
the spasticity population as well. Though the incidence in the TBI 
population is generally unknown, among those with injuries 
severe enough to require IPR, the incidence has been reported to 
be as high as 84%.683
Acute paralysis often occurs after significant damage to central 
motor pathways that occurs with TBI. This damage leaves the 
affected muscles and joints immobilized. Immobilization leads to 
reduction in longitudinal tension in a muscle and is the basis for 
muscle contracture. In animal models, 60% of muscle fiber length 
is shortened after only 24 hours of tension unloading.405 Over the 
course of a few weeks, both plastic neural and muscular reorgani-
zation can lead to muscle overactivity, or spasticity. This further 
aggravates the development of muscle and joint contractures. 230
Early identification of contracture development and spasticity 
is critical. The risk factors for spasticity development include more 
severe injury (lower GCS), motor dysfunction (hemiplegia or 
tetraplegia), associated anoxic injury, SCI, and age. Development

movement
1 Slight resistance throughout the course of the passive

can occur as early as days after injury but is typically observed 
months after injury. Patient evaluation includes a clinical and 
functional history, stretch reflex examination, range of motion 
assessment, and an active motor exam.
263 Measuring degree of 
passive abnormal muscle tone is often done with the Modified 
Ashworth Scale (MAS) ( Table 44.6). The MAS assigns a 0 to  
4 value based on the amount of resistance measured by an evalu -
ator when attempting to range a joint through an available range 
of motion. The Tardieu Scale is a true measure of spasticity, com-
paring the differences noted when a muscle is stretched at differ -
ent velocities and comparing the angles at which the catch is 
noted (Table 44.7).
408
The cornerstone of spasticity treatment is the use of static and 
dynamic splinting devices. When used in combination with pas -
sive range of motion exercises, splints serve to lengthen muscles 
and tendons that are shortening. Though there are few controlled 
trials of passive range of motion use, the pathophysiology of 
spasticity and clinical experience has largely guided use of these 
exercises. A stretching program can be easily taught to caregivers 
and transitioned to a home environment. Static splinting, such 
as serial casting, can also decrease spasticity.
557 In a setting with 
close monitoring for adverse effects, such as pressure sore devel -
opment, serial casting can be very efficacious after TBI. Caution 
should be used when initiating casting early postinjury because 
individuals may still experience high ICPs.
689 Physical modali -
ties, such as cryotherapy, superficial heat, and ultrasound, can be 
used in concert with splinting or pharmacotherapy. Multiple 
forms of electrical stimulation have been studied in the manage -
ment of spasticity, with positive results identified in some  
placebo-controlled studies.
231
Systemic pharmacologic agents help to manage spasticity in 
the TBI population. Dantrolene is indicated for the treatment of 
spasticity of CNS origin, including TBI. It acts at the sarcoplas-
mic reticulum by inhibiting calcium activity. However, dantrolene 
has been associated with hepatic toxicity, requiring regular moni-
toring of liver enzymes.
609 Baclofen acts both pre- and postsynap-
tically at the GABA-b receptors, effectively inhibiting spinal 
reflexes. Side effects include fatigue, sedation, weakness, halluci -
nations, and lowering of the seizure threshold. When studied in 
the TBI population, lower extremity Ashworth scores improved 
with baclofen use, but a similar effect was not obtained in the 
upper extremity. Somnolence limited the use of maximum dosing 
in 17% of subjects in this study.
418 Other oral agents for spasticity 
include benzodiazepines, tizanidine, and clonidine. Somnolence 
and cognitive effects often limit the usefulness of these medica-
tions in the TBI population, and they have shown some worsened 
recovery in experimental models.
182
Focal chemodenervation can be an important tool for practi -
tioners, without concerns for cognitive side effects. Phenol used 
for either motor point block or mixed nerve block can cause 
denaturation of the nerve, thus reducing focal spasticity. Advan -
tages include low cost and immediate effects. Complications 
include sensory loss, painful dysesthesias with mixed nerves, and 
vascular complication. Recent studies indicate that cryoneurolysis 
may be a safe treatment option for spasticity, with manageable 
and limited adverse effects when compared to phenol; however, 
further research is required.
667 Chemodenervation with botuli-
num toxin A and B is approved for use with both upper and lower 
extremity spasticity management. When used in combination 
with physical modalities like splinting or serial casting, improved 
range of motion and Ashworth scores can be achieved.
679
There are common patterns of upper motor neuron system 
dysfunction that occur in individuals with TBI that can be iden -
tified and effectively treated with botulinum toxin. 388 Baclofen 
can be delivered intrathecally, directly to the lumbar subarach -
noid space via an intrathecal pump. This delivery system circum-
vents the BBB and the peripheral breakdown of the orally 
administered medication. Though sedation often limits the oral 
dosing of baclofen, sedation occurs less frequently with intrathe -
cal treatment, and the dose limit is much higher. The surgical 
complications of intrathecal pump placement include infection, 
catheter dislodgement, and CSF leak or seroma. The abrupt 
withdrawal of baclofen that may occur with catheter complica
-
tions can result in rhabdomyolysis, multiple organ failure, and 
death.
270 However, when studied in a TBI population, Ashworth 
scores were improved and sustained over 1 year of treatment 
studied.
419 Animal model data suggest the effectiveness of early 
use of intrathecal baclofen (initiated 1 week after experimental 
injury) in decreasing spasticity and anxiety-like behavior without 
negative effects on cognition.
59 This technique is being transi-
tioned to human models, with safety and efficacy observed in 
implants done in the first 6 months after injury.
489 Intrathecal 
baclofen may offer cognitive benefits in certain patients with 
DoC. These cognitive improvements appear to be independent 
of its antispastic properties. Although no correlation was found

Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
Normal pressure hydrocephalus is one of the most common treat-
able neurosurgical complications following severe TBI, with an 
estimated incidence of 45%.
391 Many cases occur during the IPR 
process, and its incidence is more frequent with increased severity 
of injury and with longer rehabilitation stays.
290 Posttraumatic 
hydrocephalus is largely of the communicating variety, thus there 
is still free flow of CSF within the ventricular system. CSF absorp-
tion into the arachnoid granulations is limited by blood products, 
protein, or fibrosis, which leads to ventricular dilatation. The 
clinical presentation of acute hydrocephalus, early after injury, 
includes headache, nausea, vomiting, and lethargy. The symptoms 
of delayed hydrocephalus or normal pressure hydrocephalus are 
subtler. The clinical triad of dementia, gait ataxia, and urinary 
incontinence is described, with the gait disorder being most ame-
nable to shunting of CSF .
232 In the TBI population, this diagnosis 
should be considered for those who worsen or fail to progress 
adequately. It is necessary to perform a CT or MRI scan of the 
head without contrast to evaluate the ventricular size (Fig. 44.8). 
However, as many as 72% of individuals with severe TBI have 
been reported to have ventriculomegaly.
348 Sulcal prominence is

nal injury 1 year ago and has been experiencing worsening gait function 
and new urinary incontinence. A T2-weighted magnetic resonance imag-
ing revealed supratentorial ventriculomegaly out of proportion to the  
degree of volume loss, which is consistent with normal pressure hydro -
cephalus. The patient demonstrated a 26% improvement in the Timed Up 
and Go test following a high-volume lumbar puncture and showed clinical

often observed among individuals with cerebral atrophy and ex 
vacuo dilatation, making it less likely they will be responsive to 
shunting.303 Evaluation in questionable cases of hydrocephalus 
may require large volume CSF drainage via lumbar puncture, 
including postprocedure neurologic evaluation, as well as MRI 
cine flow studies, where spinal fluid flow through the third ven -
tricle is observed.
Paroxysmal Sympathetic Hyperactivity
PSH, otherwise known as “storming,” is a common complication 
of severe brain injury. It is a clinical diagnosis characterized by 
episodic uncontrolled surges of sympathetic and motor activity. 
Signs and symptoms include hypertension, tachycardia, diaphore-
sis, hyperthermia, tachypnea, rigidity, and dystonic posturing.
35 
The pathophysiology of the syndrome is poorly understood. Its 
characteristic sympathetic overactivity may be explained using an 
excitatory inhibitory ratio model; functional disconnections 
within the CNS may prevent inhibition from higher structures, 
thus amplifying allodynic or excitatory sensory afferent input.
34 
PSH is a diagnosis of exclusion; alternate diagnoses to consider 
include infection or sepsis, venous thromboembolism, pain (post-
operative, orthopedic, or in the setting of spasticity), withdrawal, 
neuroleptic malignant syndrome, and autonomic dysreflexia. The 
PSH Assessment Measure (PSH-AM) is an assessment tool that 
can be used to recognize and serially assess the syndrome.
35
Appropriate management of PSH is essential, as uncontrolled 
PSH is associated with increased morbidity, longer hospitaliza -
tions, delayed transfer to rehabilitation units, and increased health 
care costs.
36 T reatment of PSH primarily focuses on symptom 
management given the poorly understood pathophysiology of the 
syndrome.
416 The primary nonpharmacologic intervention is lim-
iting any physical triggers that may provoke storming episodes. 
Pharmacologic management should include both prophylactic 
and abortive strategies. Often a combination of medications with 
differing mechanisms of action is most effective, with the goal 
being to inhibit both central sympathetic outflow as well as affer-
ent sensory processing. The medications recommended for symp-
tom prevention include scheduled nonselective b-blockers  
(propranolol and labetalol), bromocriptine, dantrolene, baclofen, 
gabapentin, 
a2-agonists (clonidine), and long-acting benzodiaz -
epines. Abortive medications should be targeted to symptoms and 
should be given as needed for breakthrough episodes; medication 
options include antipyretics, antihypertensives, short-acting ben-
zodiazepines, and pain medications.
529
Endocrine Dysfunction Associated with  
Traumatic Brain Injury
Although reported frequencies and numbers vary by study,  
neuroendocrine disorders affect a significant portion of the popu-
lation with TBI, regardless of injury severity. Up to 100% of 
individuals with acute TBI also experience some type of acute 
anterior pituitary dysfunction, and up to 37% of long-term sur -
vivors continue to have anterior pituitary dysfunction.
47,623 
Growth hormone (GH) deficiency is the most common pituitary 
abnormality in adults, followed by hypogonadism, hypothyroid -
ism, hypocortisolism, and diabetes insipidus.
206 A metanalysis of  
19 studies spanning 1,137 individuals found a pooled prevalence 
of hypopituitarism in chronic TBI and aneurysmal SAH to be 
27.5%, with a higher prevalence after severe as opposed to mild 
or moderate injury.
543 Individuals involved in motor vehicle  
collisions, or whose recovery is complicated by PTS, intracranial 
hemorrhage, or cerebral contusions, are at increased risk for

pituitary dysfunction. 555 Historically, much of the pathology is 
attributed to primary and/or secondary injury to the hypothala -
mus and pituitary gland—structures that govern most neuroen -
docrine function. The hypophyseal portal vascular system that 
supplies the hypothalamic-pituitary region is vulnerable to trau -
matic injury, and frequent pituitary hemorrhage and necrosis 
have been noted in autopsy studies conducted in individuals who 
die acutely from their injuries. 58 However, contemporary studies 
suggest that hypopituitarism occurs without CT evidence of frank 
anatomic hypothalamic/pituitary injury even in msTBI. 40 Some 
literature suggests that the apparent diffusion coefficient observed 
with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) may be useful in predict-
ing hypopituitarism.696
Current guidelines do not recommend screening for endocrine 
dysfunction during acute hospitalization unless there are sugges -
tive signs or symptoms, including fatigue, weakness, low mood, 
poor concentration, decreased appetite, hypo- or hypernatremia, 
unexplained hypotension, and/or unexplained weight gain or loss. 
These symptom profiles pose challenges as they are common after 
TBI regardless of endocrinopathy. It is generally recommended to 
screen routinely for pituitary dysfunction 3 to 6 months after 
TBI, and endocrinology referral should be made if any derange -
ments persist beyond 12 months. 582 Despite these guidelines, 
some literature suggests impaired outcome at 6 and 12 months 
among those with persistent laboratory evidence of secondary 
hypogonadism during the first year postinjury.40
Both oxytocin and vasopressin are synthesized in the hypo -
thalamus and secreted by the posterior portion of the pituitary. In 
women, oxytocin functions to facilitate uterine contractions dur -
ing labor and milk excretion during lactation. Oxytocin receptors 
are present in several regions of the brain, although their impor -
tance is not fully understood. Vasopressin, also called antidiuretic 
hormone (ADH), regulates water retention. Vasopressin release is 
diurnal and has many actions on the brain, including modulating 
aggression and thermal regulation. Although less common than 
anterior pituitary dysfunction, posterior pituitary–mediated neu -
roendocrine dysfunction does occur after TBI.
Diabetes insipidus (DI) is associated with excessive thirst, 
excretion of large amounts of dilute urine that is refractory to 
fluid restriction, and elevated plasma sodium. Neurogenic DI is 
caused by ADH deficiency, and vasopressin hormone replacement 
is an effective therapy. In contrast, syndrome of inappropriate 
antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) is characterized by excessive 
ADH secretion accompanied by excess natriuresis and low plasma 
sodium. Fluid restriction and ADH inhibitors are a part of the 
therapeutic approach to this disorder. It is worth noting that 
SIADH and cerebral salt wasting (CSW) are two causes of hypo -
natremia associated with CNS pathologies that are challenging to 
diagnose and treat due to the overlap in their clinical presenta -
tions. SIADH presents in a volume expanded state because of 
ADH renal water retention and is treated by fluid restriction. 
However, CSW presents with a contracted effective arterial blood 
volume from renal salt wasting and is treated with vigorous salt 
replacement.
Several hormones affecting reproduction and metabolism are 
located in the anterior pituitary and include follicle-stimulating 
hormone, luteinizing hormone, adrenocorticotropic hormone, 
thyroid-stimulating hormone, prolactin, and GH. Early after 
injury, several aberrations in serum gonadal hormone profiles 
occur with severe TBI and are likely associated with an enhanced 
stress surge in response to the injury. T estosterone is elevated 
acutely in women and progesterone is elevated in men after 
severe TBI. After severe TBI, testosterone and progesterone lev -
els rapidly decrease in men and women (respectively), which 
refutes the long-held hypothesis generated from experimental 
models that women are afforded sex hormone–dependent neu -
roprotection.632 In addition, early rises in serum estrogens are 
indicative of significant nonneurologic systemic response to 
injury and are predictive of mortality for both men and 
women.498,502
Beyond the acute injury phase, hypogonadism is prevalent 
and is largely secondary to pituitary dysfunction, 40,623 and risk 
for this secondary condition may be related to reduced CNS 
clean-up and repair capacity associated with protective autoim -
munity.617 Among men, studies suggest that persistent secondary 
hypogonadism at 1-year postinjury can be accurately predicted as 
early as 3 to 4 months postinjury, using a two-step screening 
process, and early identification is associated with poor multidi -
mensional outcomes. 40 Additionally, menstrual cycles, 113,509 as 
well as sex hormones and cortisol, are altered for women up to 
several months after severe TBI and can have a negative impact 
on outcome. 501,509 Women who experienced concussions were 
found to have a significantly higher risk of sexual dysfunction 
compared to those with extremity injuries. In this study, con -
cussed women with sexual dysfunction also reported a greater 
number of postconcussion symptoms and worse mood scores. 23 
Primary hypogonadism can also be a consideration among those 
exposed to blast injury. 663,671
Hyperprolactinemia can affect over 50% of the population 
with TBI.491 T rauma also can lead to acute depression of the thy-
roid axis. Chronic hypothyroidism affects between 1% to 22% of 
the population with TBI and can contribute to cognitive decline.8 
Therefore thyroid function should be screened routinely, particu-
larly if recovery is slow to progress. GH deficiency in children is 
associated with short stature. In adults, GH deficiency decreases 
muscle mass, energy levels, and cognitive performance. GH defi -
ciency is identified as the most prevalent anterior pituitary  
hormone deficiency in the chronic phase following TBI, with 
incidence rates ranging from 10% to 63.6%. 313,325,544 The inci -
dence is notably lower when stringent diagnostic criteria are 
applied. The study reporting the highest incidence included cases 
of both partial and severe GH deficiency.300 Multiple reports sug-
gest chronic GH deficiency in the TBI population is correlated 
with lower QoL260,412 and increased rates of depression, insomnia, 
low libido, and weight gain. 42 Other studies suggest impaired 
aerobic capacity with GH deficiency after TBI. 435 However, GH 
replacement can have a beneficial impact on recovery as well as 
other factors such as physical strength, cognition, psychological 
health, body composition, and QoL.329
Posttraumatic Headache
PTH is the most common complaint following head injury, 
accompanying 30% to 90% of TBIs.437 According to the Interna-
tional Headache Society Classification,  Third Edition, 5 PTHs are 
secondary headaches related to either minor, moderate, or severe 
TBI, whiplash, or craniotomy. PTHs are considered acute in the 
first 3 months following presentation, after which they are consid-
ered persistent.5 Despite their etiology and temporal relation there 
are no distinguishing features or management strategies between 
PTH and other primary headache types, namely migraine and 
tension-type headaches.
While many PTHs respond well acutely to occasional use of 
over-the-counter analgesics such as acetaminophen or NSAIDs,146 
care should be taken to avoid overuse that risks development of

medication overuse headaches (MOH). The risk for MOH is high 
with use of opioid-containing medications; however, practitioners 
may prefer these drugs to others like NSAIDs early after more 
severe injuries where bleeding may be a consideration. Other use-
ful agents for acute treatment of PTH include serotonin receptor 
agonists (triptans), ergot derivatives, and antiemetics.46 Consider-
ation should be made with use of triptans and ergots early after 
injury when cerebral pressure regulation may be impaired. PTH 
variants presenting as migraine or tension-type headaches should 
be treated similarly to how those entities are treated in the absence 
of TBI. A combined prophylactic and abortive regimen is recom-
mended for PTH with migrainoid features after careful evaluation 
for potential drug interactions or medical history contraindica -
tions. Prophylactic agents can include b-blockers, calcium  
channel blockers, anticonvulsants, and tricyclic antidepressants. 
Starting doses should be low and, if responsive, gradually increased 
as tolerated. Botulinum toxin type A has been shown to signifi -
cantly improve the frequency and severity of PTHs in military 
veterans compared to a placebo and is an option for PTH preven-
tion.698 Many classes of preventative drugs can have significant side 
effect profiles in the postacute and chronic phases of injury. Thus 
practitioners may need to tailor therapy to minimize adverse effects 
on other recovery domains, and treatment selection may differ 
depending on factors such as time from injury, age, and premorbid 
headache history, as well as concurrent cervicalgia, cognitive/
behavioral dysfunction, and mood disorders, among others.
Cognition After Traumatic Brain Injury
Cognitive deficits are among the most debilitating and complex 
aspects of TBI to manage and treat. Cognitive problems after 
TBI can span several neuropsychological domains, including 
arousal, attention, memory, and executive control. The neurobio-
logic basis of both physiologic cognitive function and TBI-
induced dysfunction are not completely understood, and neuro -
pharmacologic approaches for treating TBI-mediated deficits 
have largely drawn upon literature from related disorders that 
present with cognitive dysfunction. Among these are attention 
deficit-hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), Parkinson disease, nar -
colepsy, and Alzheimer disease/dementia. However, ongoing 
work has increasingly shown that the patho-mechanisms involved 
in TBI-related cognitive deficits are unique and are not wholly 
like these other diseases.
Arousal is one of the most basic cognitive functions required to 
engage in other higher levels of cognition. Arousal is governed by 
monoamine release from the reticular activating system, a network 
of nuclei originating in the brainstem and thalamus. DA originates 
from the ventral tegmentum and the pars compacta of the substan-
tia nigra. Histamine originates from the tuberomammillary 
nucleus, serotonin originates from the median and dorsal raphe 
nuclei, and norepinephrine is released from the locus coeruleus, 
with DA thought to be central to this function.39 Fundamental to 
injury-induced reductions in arousal is impaired consciousness. 
Widespread cortical deafferentation injury impacts structures rel-
evant to the meso-circuit and is thought to be a central reason why 
individuals with severe TBI and/or HIBI can present with DoC. 
Specifically, injury-induced meso-circuit disruptions can result 
from reductions in thalamic projection outflow needed to modu-
late striatal medium spiny neuron (MSN) firing. Reduced inhibi-
tory tone from striatal MSN projections increases pallidothalamic 
inhibitory tone, which in turn decreases the thalamocortical  
activation needed to support consciousness.214,540
Attention can refer to a wide variety of different cognitive 
processes that are both voluntary and involuntary. In addition, 
attention is a widely distributed cognitive function that involves 
both cortical and subcortical pathways including striatal and tha -
lamic inputs and reticular activation. However, it is generally 
accepted that DA plays a significant role in attention.67 Attention 
can be operationalized to include multiple processes. Sensory gat-
ing is a preattentive process that filters incoming stimuli to limit 
information into attention-processing networks. 463 Selective 
attention involves the ability to direct cognitive resources to a 
salient stimulus, while sustained attention allows for maintenance 
of attentional processing on a specific stimulus. Like arousal, there 
is overlap of many of these attentional processes with other cogni-
tive functions such as memory and executive functioning (EF). 
Further, attentional deficits can contribute to dysfunction in 
other cognitive domains.
Relative to arousal and attention, the anatomy involved with 
memory is better characterized, with specific memory processing 
events (e.g., retrieval, consolidation) ascribed to specific brain 
regions. For example, damage to the hippocampus has historically 
been associated with reproducible deficits in spatial and temporal 
memory processing. 76 Memory involves both encoding and 
retrieval processes, each of which may involve different structures. 
Explicit learning and memory are considered to be hippocampal-
dependent processes, a major form of which is declarative memory, 
or memory for facts and events that can be consciously discussed. 
Declarative memory requires the hippocampus for memory con -
solidation and utilizes the frontal cortex for memory retrieval. 
Declarative memory retrieval is associative in nature in that any 
area of the brain involved in encoding the information can be 
tapped to help initiate retrieval. In contrast, implicit learning and 
memory is a hippocampal-independent process that involves sub-
consciously learning how to do something. Procedural memory is 
a common type of implicit learning. Procedural learning and 
memory have a relatively inflexible retrieval process in that it is 
limited specifically to the processes involved in learning the task.252 
However, strategy formulation, another relevant form of implicit 
learning, has shown relatively good carryover effects to a range of 
tasks.135 Implicit learning and memory are relevant for many other 
types of cognitive functions, and both experimental and clinical 
data show that, compared to explicit memory, implicit learning 
and memory networks are relatively intact after TBI. 541 As such, 
the implicit learning and memory network capacity is commonly 
leveraged in cognitive training programs to produce guided 
responses that facilitate functional improvement in other domains 
such as ADLs. 541 Because of the central role that the hippocampus 
plays in memory, cholinergic systems projecting to the hippocam-
pus are implicated in memory dysfunction. The prefrontal cortex 
(PFC) and corticostriatal DA signaling system, in addition to the 
hippocampus, are also important for memory formation.
EF encompasses numerous functions, but it is generally 
defined as the capacity to organize, plan, execute, and change 
cognitive functions. EF is needed for abstraction, problem-  
solving, self-direction, systematic memory searching and retrieval, 
and moving from one set of information to another. It is also used 
when maintaining information and generating goals and con -
cepts. Working memory (WM) commonly refers to those cognitive 
processes that provide the capacity to both maintain and manipu-
late a limited amount of information over a brief period of time. 
WM is closely related to EF in that both processes involve  
the PFC. DA projections to the PFC, along with PFC-striatal 
networks, are required for normal EF .252
