Clinical Neuropharmacology for Treating 
Traumatic Brain Injury–Mediated Cognitive 
Deficits
Pharmacotherapy is employed for the cognitive deficits associated 
with brain injuries under the hypothesis that increasing levels of 
the neurotransmitters that mediate those cognitive functions can 
improve them.
288 Fig. 44.9 and (Video 44.1) provide a graphical 
overview of selected common agents and their mechanisms of 
action within the meso-circuit model described earlier.
Although amphetamine has been used to some degree clini -
cally as both a DA and an NE agent, methylphenidate (MPH) 
is more widely accepted as an effective neurostimulant to 
improve cognition. According to current evidence-based guide -
lines, MPH is recommended as a neurostimulant to improve 
cognition, particularly attention. Much of the evidence for this 
treatment guideline is derived from small but well-controlled 
clinical trials showing improvements in cognitive processing 
speed and caregiver ratings of attention,
658 as well as trials com -
bining MPH with cognitive training paradigms. 396 MPH  
acts primarily at the DAT level, blocking the reuptake of DA 
and norepinephrine, subsequently increasing extracellular neu -
rotransmitter levels.
,27,278,355
Amantadine is a weak NMDA glutamate receptor antagonist 
that decreases glutamatergic neurotransmission, in addition to 
indirectly increasing DA release and decreasing DA reuptake.
355 
Evidence-based guidelines support the use of amantadine to 
enhance general cognition and attention in individuals with 
msTBI. Previous clinical reports demonstrate improved scores on 
ADL scales in case reports of individuals treated with amanta -
dine.
675,692 Case reports also indicate general improvements in 
global functioning and processing speed.90,327 PET imaging studies 
indicate amantadine-mediated increases in cortical glucose metab-
olism in patients with TBI.328 Notably, a large clinical trial demon-
strated the benefits of amantadine in individuals with DoC who 
were in the subacute-to-early chronic phases of recovery.
216 In 
patients with DAI, amantadine appears to be effective in improv-
ing cognition independent of the timing of administration.417
Bromocriptine is less well studied compared to MPH and 
amantadine. Bromocriptine’s effects are complex as a partial D1 
antagonist and mixed D2 agonist-antagonist.
354,583 Nonhuman 
studies suggest low-dose bromocriptine can act as a partial D2 
antagonist and facilitate presynaptic DA release.
65,354 Small case 
series show that low-dose bromocriptine may improve EF . 397 
However, previous work suggests that daily bromocriptine is not 
effective for treating attentional deficits.
660

987Chapter 44 Traumatic Brain Injury
Aside from standard monoaminergic agents noted above, other 
work suggests additional agents may also influence arousal and 
consciousness. Clinical reports suggest some potential efficacy 
with improving arousal using the selective norepinephrine trans -
porter inhibitor atomoxetine.507 Modafinil may regulate the sleep-
wake cycle and improve hypoarousal by impacting multiple brain 
regions,38,355 though its molecular mechanisms of action are not 
well defined. However, some literature suggests modafinil has 
modulatory actions on the locus coeruleus and PFC, 426 while 
other studies suggest modafinil influences GABA release within 
multiple meso-circuit structures included within the nigro-  
striatal-pallidal pathway.185 Recent studies suggest that pallidal 
GABAA receptor binding with zolpidem, which has a high affinity 
for a1 subunit–containing GABAA receptors, may increase meso-
circuit connectivity after brain injury, supporting the pallidal-
thalamo-cortical activity needed to improve consciousness and 
facilitate other areas of function.454,486
Beyond arousal and consciousness, both case series and small 
RCTs suggest that cholinesterase inhibitors can improve memory 
and attention as well as general cognition. 553,643 Interestingly, 
daily treatment with the cholinesterase inhibitor donepezil results 
in cortical increases in metabolism, and magnitude of metabolic 
change is associated with degree of clinical treatment response.304 
A recent multicenter, randomized, blinded, placebo-controlled 
trial demonstrated that donepezil has the potential to significantly 
improve memory versus placebo in predominantly severe TBI 
with a number needed to treat of 3.5. 24 Donepezil may also have 
some capacity after HIBI to improve arousal and therapy partici -
pation in the acute care and IPR settings, 485 possibly through its 
modulatory effects on striatal medium spiny projection neurons 
associated with the meso-circuit.
CNS inflammatory processes are well known to diminish 
DA,183 cholinergic,685 serotonergic,685 and noradrenergic 159 neu-
rotransmitter systems within the brain. Further, recent studies 
also suggest that chronic systemic inflammatory burden may also 
diminish cognitive function among those with moderate and 
severe TBI.424 Given the central role that inflammation plays in 
chronic TBI pathophysiology and global recovery, 616 these data 
suggest that antiinflammatory strategies may be a fruitful line of 
future research in treating cognitive dysfunction after TBI.
The Purpose of the Inpatient 
Neuropsychological Assessment
There are multiple roles for neuropsychological testing across the 
course of recovery from TBI. As soon as a patient starts to emerge 
from a DoC state and transit PTA, the neuropsychologist can 
assess the patient to determine whether other comorbid condi -
tions are contributing to their presentation, characterize the 
nature of the cognitive and behavioral deficits to inform the treat-
ment plan, and evaluate the effectiveness of said treatment plan. 
The neuropsychologist will assess the patient’s ability to engage 
with testing and then flexibly utilize various assessment tools to 
first gain general information about their neurocognitive func -
tioning and then evaluate areas of interest in greater detail. 562 
Although the barriers posed by medical needs and impairments in 
endurance and attention must be navigated, sufficiently brief 
assessments can be feasibly implemented.289
There are multitudinous neuropsychological assessments, with 
some seeing reliable use on the inpatient brain injury rehabilita -
tion unit. Because all services may not be fortunate enough to 
have a neuropsychologist, these tools can be performed by Allied 
Health, including OTs and SLPs. The JFK CRS-R is the most 
used tool to assess patients serially while in DoC and is discussed 
in that section of this chapter. Once sufficiently awake and in 
PTA, the O-Log and GOAT are used to monitor recovery of 
memory. Consistent scores of 25 on the O-Log or 75 on the 
GOAT denote emergence from PTA. Agitation can be measured 
by the Agitated Behavior Scale, which has subscales for disinhibi-
tion, aggression, and lability, and when monitored throughout 
the day, it can provide valuable information about the nature of a 
patient’s agitation and what factors may be influencing it.117 The 
Repeatable Battery for the Assessment of Neuropsychological 
Status (RBANS) is a more comprehensive battery to assess general 
cognitive functioning.233,289
Practical Interventions for Cognitive Impairment 
Following Traumatic Brain Injury
When interacting with patients during the IPR phase of recovery, 
it is helpful to understand the “typical” presentation of cognitive 
impairment associated with TBI. Many individuals who partici -
pate in IPR following msTBI typically are still in PTA. During 
this period of confusion, individuals may be disoriented to per -
son, place, time, and circumstance. They generally do not remem-
ber what has happened to them and do not process and retain 
new information. As a result, it may be very difficult for them to 
make sense out of their surroundings and circumstances. They 
find themselves uncomfortable and cannot process what items are 
important and necessary for their care. They may refuse therapies 
because they are tired and in pain and cannot appreciate the ben-
efits of participation. In addition, they will likely have difficulties 
remembering multistep instructions in therapy and what was 
presented to them during a previous session or perhaps even 
moments before. These individuals, likely, also have additional 
cognitive impairments caused by their injury. Examples may 
include language deficits, sensory impairments, difficulty initiat -
ing or stopping behaviors, or difficulties interpreting visual or 
spatial information. Individuals may wander or engage in inap -
propriate, unsafe, and repetitive behaviors.
As patients are often not able to make sense of their environ -
ment during this period of acute confusion, an overarching goal of 
the rehabilitation program should be to provide a structured pro-
gram to assist patients through this period of recovery. As recovery 
progresses, they will learn what to expect from their daily schedule 
more easily if structure has been established. In addition, orienta-
tion testing to assess progress through PTA, therapists, nursing 
staff, and family members can also gently provide orientation 
information in conversation throughout the day. It is often helpful 
to provide a daily calendar and clock in the patient’s room for easy 
reference. Staff members should reintroduce themselves each time 
they interact with the individual, even if they have worked with 
the individual recently, until they are able to reliably and indepen-
dently identify individuals. As many individuals are sensitive to 
sensory stimulation or may have field cuts or visual neglect follow-
ing TBI, staff members are advised to approach individuals from 
the front, gain their attention, and explain procedures prior to 
initiating physical contact, moving them, or engaging in other 
interactions. These strategies will limit the likelihood of staff mem-
bers startling the individual. Medical procedures and therapy tasks 
should be explained in a step-by-step manner, even if individuals 
do not respond or show overt awareness.
Generally, an individual who has sustained a TBI does not lose 
biographical memory, and so can recall information about their

family and personal history, even though their ability to encode 
and recall more recent events is impaired. As a result, it is often 
comforting to the individual to have pictures of family members, 
pets, favorite places, and so forth, in their hospital room. It is 
helpful for therapists to ask family members for detailed informa-
tion about the individual’s history (i.e., education, work history, 
hobbies, interests) so that components of these aspects of the 
individual’s life may be incorporated into therapies where possible 
so that therapy can be more personalized. Individuals who are not 
aware of their deficits or are impulsive may be at risk for engaging 
in unsafe behaviors. Initially, the best approach is to guard them 
through therapeutic interactions. One-to-one sitters or restraints 
may be required for safety. During recovery, it is helpful to teach 
step-by-step strategies to reduce impulsivity and compare perfor -
mance and progress to an objective standard. Logical verbal dis -
cussion is often not effective when a lack of awareness is due to 
cognitive impairment related to injury. Group therapy with peers 
who may challenge the individual can be effective at later stages 
of recovery.
Behavioral and cognitive issues discussed here tend to improve 
over the recovery period. That said, some issues observed in the 
inpatient setting may continue to be present with transition to 
home or other setting. In fact, according to one study, approxi -
mately 40% of individuals reported ongoing unmet needs at  
1 year after injury, with the most frequent issues cited including 
issues with memory and problem solving, managing stress and 
emotional upset, and temper control. 120 However, families can 
learn many of the therapy routines and techniques and apply 
them in the home setting. Continuing to set routines and struc -
ture to assist individuals will increase the likelihood of success and 
may decrease depression, irritability, and difficulties with explo -
sive anger. T reatment with medication combined with therapy 
addresses adjustment to disability, anxiety, and depression, though 
self-management skills are also recommended. Box 44.4 reviews 
helpful behavioral and cognitive strategies for common issues fol-
lowing TBI.
Behavioral and Mood Problems
It is common for individuals to experience behavioral and mood 
changes that can interfere with their rehabilitation and decrease 
their QoL.
Agitation
Agitation is a common behavioral phenomenon during the acute 
phase of recovery from TBI, occurring in about a third of patients 
with msTBI.267 A significant obstacle, agitation poses challenges 
to acute care, rehabilitation staff, and patient’s family and friends; 
increases length of stay; and decreases likelihood of discharge to 
the community.267 While there are various specific definitions, 
most physiatrists in one survey emphasized increased psychomo -
tor activity, impulsivity, impaired attention, perseveration, anger, 
physical and/or verbal aggression, and disorganized thinking as 
key components of agitation. Additionally, while traditionally 
associated with PTA (and its own level of the Rancho Los Amigos 
Scale, Level IV), there is no consensus and thus inconsistent use 
of the term agitation to describe similar behaviors in patients who 
are no longer disoriented.201
Neurobehaviorally, agitation is thought to result from the con-
fluence of disorientation and impaired processing of stimuli, peak-
ing while those faculties are improving yet still severely impaired 
and then fading as the patient emerges from PTA. 400 There is 
mixed evidence for associations between frontotemporal lesions 
and agitation.57,267 An analysis of over 2,000 patients with acute 
IPR found that cognitive functioning was inversely correlated with 
severity of agitation, and that infection also strongly predicted 
agitation.57 Like infection, there are myriad other medical comor-
bidities that can worsen or mimic agitation, including seizures, 
constipation, urinary retention, wounds, and medication side 
effects (Table 44.8). These entities can directly affect neurologic 
functioning, or a patient’s difficulty communicating can lead to 
increased distress and in that way cause agitation, and it is for this 
Helpful Environmental and Cognitive Interventions for Working With Individuals With Traumatic 
Brain Injury
Early in recovery, individuals with brain injury are often confused and have dif -
ficulty remembering new information. Staff should:
• Introduce and reintroduce themselves, even if they have done it earlier 
in the day or week.
• Provide consistency and structure (ideally a consistent schedule with 
consistent staff).
• Provide reorientation frequently.
• Use simple, short sentences and don’t give too much information at once.
• Cue the patient as needed, providing some direction and guidance.
• Provide extra time and repetition as needed.
Individuals with brain injury may become easily overstimulated. Overstimulation 
will likely have a negative impact on cognition and may increase the likelihood 
of irritable or agitated behaviors. It can be helpful to:
• Limit distractions and noise around the patient as needed (move treat -
ment to a quiet location, dim lights, reduce noises such as television 
and radio, limit the number of people talking and interacting with the 
person at one time).
• Break down difficult tasks into smaller steps and give simple, step-by-
step directions.
• Be flexible in treatment: Redirect patients when they become frustrated 
with an activity, allow rest breaks, alternate activities as needed.
• Remain calm in interactions and speak in a clear, concise manner using 
quiet volume and calming tones.
• Keep issues such as pain and fatigue in mind, and treat as needed.
Individuals with brain injury are often “impulsive.” This means they may act 
without thinking. For example, patients may move quickly and unsafely and try 
to do things that they are not capable of doing independently. To promote 
safety:
• Be sure to have the patient’s attention before you speak to them.
• Speak slowly, clearly, and softly using the patient’s name frequently to 
keep their attention.
• Use demonstration in addition to verbal instructions when possible.
• Promote team and family communication so that consistent strategies 
are being employed.
Additional tips for working with individuals with brain injury:
• Always approach patients slowly and gently and approach them from 
the front, not from behind.
• Don’t take behaviors personally.
• During periods of significant agitation, create situations in which the 
patient can be successful and avoid situations in which they may fail or 
will need to be corrected.

Measures of Agitation
The behaviors associated with agitation have been most effec -
tively described in the Agitated Behavior Scale ( Box 44.5). 117 
This scale can be helpful for monitoring the progression through 
recovery, as well as for evaluation of the effectiveness of inter -
ventions such as medication or behavior plans to manage agita -
tion. In addition, valuable information may be gained regarding

Treatment of Agitation
A personalized, multimodal approach to agitation should be 
implemented for every patient. As discussed previously, attention 
to contributing factors should be given first. Then, nonpharmaco-
logic options such as environmental modification and behavioral 
plans are encouraged, despite a lack of robust evidence, because of 
their adaptability and favorable risk profile in comparison to 
pharmacologic interventions.
267,360 Medications can be and are 
utilized for agitation with careful consideration to side effects and 
the risk for negative influence on the patient’s larger recovery 
trajectory (Table 44.9).
A behavioral evaluation and plan may be a useful approach as 
an alternative initial approach to pharmacologic management. 
Generally, in conducting a behavioral evaluation, observations of 
what happens immediately before and after a problem behavior 
are recorded to ascertain possible unintended triggers and/or 
consequences that influence the individual’s behavior. Patterns of 
antecedents, behaviors, and consequences that are observed may 
be used in creating an individualized behavior plan to promote 
desired behavior. Physical restraints should be minimized,
360 but 
when unavoidable, the least restrictive device should be applied. 
For example, an enclosure bed provides the restless patient with 
freedom of movement that limb restraints would not. Similarly, 
select patients with tracheostomy or gastrostomy tubes might be 
manageable with mitts rather than wrist restraints to prevent 
interference with those devices. Wheelchair seatbelts that fasten 
from behind can prevent patients from getting up and falling.

991Chapter 44 Traumatic Brain Injury
Neuroleptic medications are frequently used for acute agita -
tion when patient or staff safety is of concern and are appreciated 
for their calming and sedating effect and ability to be injected. 
While thought to be preferable to typical neuroleptics with their 
strong dopamine antagonism, atypical neuroleptics such as olan -
zapine, ziprasidone, risperidone, and quetiapine have also been 
shown to have negative cognitive and physical effects on patients 
with TBI in the short and longer terms. 265,479 Similarly to neuro-
leptics, benzodiazepines, GABA A receptor agonists, are used to 
manage agitation when sedation is desired, most often in the 
critical care setting, but they carry significant side effects that 
make their employment rare in the rehabilitation setting. 267
There is less robust evidence for the use of depression, anxiety, 
and sleep medications for the treatment of TBI-related agitation. 
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and trazodone have some 
evidence for reducing agitation in dementia populations, and 
there are case studies describing benefit with buspirone and ami -
triptyline.267
Leaning on the conceptualization that agitation is rooted in 
confusion and decreased processing speed, there is a rationale for 
the use of psychostimulants to attempt to improve those problems 
and in so doing diminish agitation. Despite this, evidence for 
stimulants such as methylphenidate for agitation is sparse, 267,429 
and appetite to potentially exacerbate agitated behaviors with a 
sympathomimetic stimulant should be strategic, focusing on 
patient-specific factors that may affect stimulant efficacy (e.g., 
underlying history of ADHD).
Sleep-Wake Disturbances
Problems with fatigue and sleep are common across the TBI sever-
ity spectrum and timeline and can cause symptoms that degrade 
multiple aspects of function. Sleep-wake disorders can include 
insomnia, excessive fatigue or sleepiness, and abnormal nocturnal 
behaviors.124,528 Up to 50% of patients with TBI describe some 
form of insomnia. 464 While insomnia rarely occurs without 
fatigue, fatigue is more often to occur without insomnia, and is 
often not relieved by sleep. 83 Sleep patterns after TBI are charac -
terized by decreased rapid eye movement (REM) and slow wave 
sleep. T otal sleep time and efficiency are also disturbed.112 A com-
prehensive treatment plan for TBI-related sleep and fatigue 
should include assessment for potential contributing factors and 
then behavioral, environmental, and medical interventions based 
on individual needs.508
Fatigue can result from poor sleep, but post-TBI fatigue is 
regarded as clinically distinct from sleepiness, and it is thought to 
be the result of disrupted connectivity between key neural struc -
tures, including the basal ganglia, amygdala, thalamus, and fron -
tal cortex.93 Insomnia is often considered to be either of the sleep 
onset or maintenance variety, although both can occur in the 
same patient. Sleep onset insomnia can be idiopathic, due to poor 
sleep hygiene or a distracting sleep environment, or due to comor-
bid psychiatric conditions such as anxiety or depression or related 
to a movement disorder such as restless leg syndrome. Sleep main-
tenance insomnia can also be caused by movement disorders, 
pain, environmental disruption, or obstructive sleep apnea. 528
A comprehensive sleep assessment includes attention to the 
patient’s past medical history, accumulated comorbidities in their 
history of present illness, medications, and the details of their 
sleep itself and their sleep environment. Because this patient 
population frequently has problems with communication, collat -
eral information gathering is critical, and the thoughtful examiner 
should consult with the patient’s family and overnight staff and 
consider objective measures of sleep, such as sleep logging, actig -
raphy, polysomnography, and multiple sleep latency testing.371,528 
Physical examination should attend to the patient’s habitus and 
cranial nerve function, as obesity or cranial nerve palsy can drive 
hypoventilation syndromes due to physical difficulty breathing or 
damage to the brainstem centers that moderate sleep, including 
the tegmentum and medulla.528
Sleep-wake disorder management should begin with nonphar-
macologic intervention.596 Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is 
the gold standard for insomnia, which sets out to diminish prob -
lematic behaviors and beliefs surrounding sleep and is as effective 
as pharmacologic interventions in the short term and more effec -
tive in the long term. The pillars of CBT include stimulus control 
(associating bed and bedroom with sleep and limiting other 
activities when trying to sleep), sleep restriction (sleeping in a nar-
row window that progressively widens), cognitive therapy (shap -
ing problematic beliefs and attitudes surrounding sleep), sleep 
hygiene (education on best health practices that have been shown 
to improve sleep, including diet, exercise, avoiding toxic sub -
stances, and environmental optimization), and fatigue manage -
ment (pacing oneself and managing endurance). 157 Other  
nonpharmacologic interventions with evidence for efficacy 
include light therapy and acupuncture.468
After environmental and behavioral optimization and atten -
tion to medical and pharmacologic contributors to sleep-wake 
disorders, pharmacologic interventions may be considered. 157 
Care should be taken in choosing sleep medications to start at low 
doses and titrate them slowly. There are certain classes of medica-
tions that are typically avoided in the TBI population due to 
unfavorable potential side effect profiles. GABA A agonists like 
benzodiazepines have been demonstrated to cause memory and 
cognitive impairment in healthy populations and are not rou -
tinely used for patients with TBI. T ricyclic antidepressants are to 
be avoided in msTBIs due to their anticholinergic activity and 
potential to lower the seizure threshold, and histamine blockers 
like diphenhydramine are similarly avoided for their anticholiner-
gic activity.193
The nonbenzodiazepine GABAA agonist “Z” drugs, including 
zolpidem, zaleplon, and eszopiclone, are commonly used in the 
TBI population and have fewer cognitive side effects and less risk 
for dependency.157 Melatonin is an endogenous hormone that has 
been shown to have reduced production in a study of patients 
with TBI and can improve sleep-wake cycles and daytime alert -
ness.236,425 Mirtazapine is an atypical antidepressant that acts on 
serotonin, histamine, and a-1 adrenergic receptors. At low doses, 
it acts as a hypnotic, promoting sleep, while at higher doses, it 
becomes more stimulating. Its side effect of weight gain makes it 
convenient for depressed patients who are not sleeping or eat -
ing.193 T razodone is another atypical antidepressant that also 
works on serotonin, histamine, and a-1 adrenergic receptors and 
is hypnotic at lower doses.193 Atypical antipsychotics like quetiap-
ine and olanzapine are also used for sleep-wake disorders but with 
care due to their dopamine antagonism. Quetiapine is the least 
dopamine antagonistic of the atypical antipsychotics and is a 
potent histamine antagonist, and so it is most used for sleep.
Affective Disorders after TBI
The risk for mood disorders is elevated for all severities of TBI. 
Depression and anxiety are most common, with PTSD, panic 
disorder, and agoraphobia also occurring.269 While the presence of

a premorbid psychiatric disorder strongly predicts the develop -
ment of an affective disorder, they can also occur on their own, 
and their incidence decreases as time goes on from injury. 16
Posttraumatic Depression (PTD)
Depression is the most common psychological problem after 
TBI.174 The prevalence of depression after TBI is 6% to 77%. 330 
The variation in prevalence rates is due to different measurements 
used to assess depression, the time course when assessed after 
injury, and differences in injury severity. The prevalence of post -
traumatic depression (PTD) is highest in the first year after 
injury,27 affecting over 70% of those with moderate-to-severe 
injury within the first 6 months postinjury, but delayed depres -
sion is not uncommon.237 PTD is likely multifactorial, stemming 
in various degrees from the pathophysiologic consequences of the 
head injury itself, a premorbid predisposition to depression, and/
or a postinjury psychological response to the trauma. 516 Depres-
sion may result from the biochemical changes in the brain follow-
ing injury. Medications to treat depression can be beneficial for 
some,175,674 though according to animal models, antidepressant 
response rates may be lower in the setting of inflammation, 481 
which is known to occur chronically after TBI. 333 Several poten-
tial risk factors affect postinjury depression rates. Minority status, 
unemployment, low income, history of alcohol abuse, and low 
education levels are associated with PTD. 516 There is no clear 
consensus concerning how preinjury psychiatric history, 184,283 
age,223,284,670 gender,283,284,346,670 and injury severity516 affect PTD 
rates. One study suggests rates of over 70% PTD for those with a 
premorbid history of mood disorders, while PTD rates are much 
lower for those without premorbid history. 168 More recent evi -
dence suggests that the acute inflammatory response mounted as 
a result of secondary injury, 286 chronic systemic inflammatory 
burden,30 as well as genetic background 168 may influence PTD 
risk, giving rise to the concept of stratified treatment trials and 
exploration of antiinflammatory agents as therapies for PTD 
treatment.
The overlap between depressive symptoms and symptoms of 
neurologic disease presents a challenge when assessing PTD. 
Overlapping symptoms can include insomnia, irritability, and 
lack of motivation. Depression has been assessed in the TBI 
population using a variety of measurements, including the Zung 
Self Rating Depression Scale, Hamilton Rating Scale for Depres -
sion, and Beck Depression Inventory.179,363,545 The DSM-5 criteria 
for identifying major depression have also been used to assess 
depression in patients with TBI. 3 The Patient Health Question -
naire (PHQ-9), which is based on DSM-5 criteria of depression, 
has been used to assess depression in the TBI population.173 PTD 
is associated with poor outcome after injury.174 Depression is also 
associated with impaired cognitive function like psychomotor 
slowing and decreased information processing speed, memory, 
and flexibility in problem solving,516 providing some rationale for 
PTD-associated cognitive impairment after TBI.174
Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
There is considerable debate regarding PTSD following TBI; this 
has gained significant attention considering combat and blast-
related injuries related to recent conflicts in Afghanistan and Iraq.
It was previously thought that amnesia for the traumatic event 
was a protective mechanism, and that PTSD, which is an emo -
tional response to a traumatic event, could not occur for an event 
that is not able to be remembered.389 More recently, the literature 
suggests that evidence from the conflicts in Iraq and Afghanistan 
has demonstrated that TBI is in fact a risk factor for PTSD to 
occur, and that it occurs at all levels of injury. 406,584 The severity 
of the injury may have some protective qualities in modulating 
PTSD development.306 For example, it is less likely for PTSD to 
occur in instances where significant loss of consciousness and 
amnesia for the event occur.277
In comparison, it may be more likely for an individual with no 
or brief loss of consciousness, such as with mild TBI, to develop 
PTSD due to the increased likelihood of recall for the injury event 
or events leading up to or following injury. Some individuals with 
msTBI go on to develop symptoms of PTSD; however, the exact 
rates are unclear. 306 In those that do develop PTSD symptoms, 
very few report re-experience of the event as a symptom. How -
ever, some individuals, despite having no actual memory of the 
event, may develop an unconscious/implicit fear response. 343 
Individuals may also develop an emotional response to informa -
tion that is provided to them about the event, such as stories or 
pictures relayed by others, 251 or in response to brief periods of 
memory during the confusing period of PTA.305
The overlap in symptoms of PTSD and TBI is significant and 
complicates the diagnostic process. 584 Additional personality fac -
tors, psychosocial factors, and attributions about the injury itself 
can impact the likelihood of PTSD symptoms emerging after 
TBI.218,266,307 Research regarding TBI and PTSD in military vet -
erans adds additional caveats to what is understood about PTSD 
after injury. Some data suggest that both mild TBI and PTSD are 
more prevalent when injuries are a result of IEDs, 431 or more 
generally, from blasts. 518,519 However, additional work has not 
found a difference in PTSD incidence in blast versus nonblast-
related TBI.368
TBI and Suicide
There are associations between TBI and suicidality and risk for 
suicide in the civilian and military populations.269 Although high 
quality studies control for premorbid affective disorders in making 
these determinations, comorbid depression and PTSD are signifi-
cant risk factors for suicide after TBI.398 This is similar to the gen-
eral population, where conditions such as depression and PTSD 
also increase the risk of suicide.74,575 One study suggests that both 
systemic inflammation and also impulsivity contribute significantly 
to suicidal risk in a cohort of individuals with msTBI.285
Neurodegenerative Disorders, Chronic 
Traumatic Encephalopathy, and Traumatic 
Encephalopathy Syndrome
There is an increasing understanding that TBI is associated with 
increased risk for neurodegenerative disease development later in 
life. Although previously thought to be a risk only for more 
severely injured individuals, head injuries of any severity have 
been shown to increase the risk of all-cause dementia by 63% to 
96%. Mechanistically, it has been hypothesized and explored in 
animal models that the etiology of this development may be 
related to chronic inflammation, excitotoxicity, gliosis, neuro -
trophin dysfunction, and amyloid plaque and tau deposition. 69
Although “punch drunkenness” saw colloquial use in boxing 
beforehand, it was first described as a clinical syndrome associated

with repetitive head trauma by Martland in 1928. 381 A patholo -
gist by training, Martland described the cases of 23 boxers of 
differing styles and skill levels who exhibited signs and symptoms, 
including parkinsonism, balance problems, tremor, and cognitive 
impairment.92 Later renamed dementia pugilistica and then chronic 
traumatic encephalopathy  (CTE), this syndrome saw a sharp 
increase in visibility after Omalu first described the neuropatho -
logic protein deposition of CTE in 2005 in a deceased retired 
professional American football player, who exhibited cognitive 
impairment, mood disorder, and parkinsonism. 461 Further work 
has characterized the pathognomonic lesion of CTE to be patchy 
hyperphosphorylated tau deposition into neurofibrillary tangles, 
neurites, and less commonly, astrocytes around the small blood 
vessels deep in the cortical sulci. 401,402 These brain regions have 
been shown through computational modeling to be vulnerable to 
shear forces.213 It is not clearly understood how tau protein accu -
mulates and spreads as it does; hypotheses include axonal injury, 
transsynaptic spread, and neuroinflammation. 100 There is a rela -
tionship between the extent of exposure to repetitive head injuries 
(RHI) and severity of CTE findings.404
T erminology addressing the clinical syndrome associated with 
CTE coalesced around traumatic encephalopathy syndrome 
(TES) in 2014 with the development of preliminary diagnostic 
criteria.428 In 2021, the National Institute of Neurological Disor-
ders and Stroke (NINDS) Consensus Diagnostic Criteria for TES 
were formed by an expert panel with a goal to make them more 
specific for delineating CTE from other diseases with similar fea -
tures, which was a common problem in the literature of the ensu-
ing period. 340 The four diagnostic criteria agreed upon were:  
(1) significant exposure to RHIs; (2) core clinical features of cog -
nitive impairment, neurobehavioral dysregulation, and a progres-
sive course; (3) not fully accountable to another disorder; and  
(4) degree of functional impairment.295
The 2021 NINDS criteria for TES were created to guide future 
research for the understanding of this disease and the develop -
ment of premortem biomarkers for CTE, and as such clinical 
diagnosis of TES is not recommended. Furthermore, premortem 
diagnosis of CTE should not be given either. Despite removal of 
mood disorders from TES core criteria, comorbid depression and 
anxiety have been shown to have strong association with premor-
tem CTE diagnosis, along with sleep apnea. 160 A patient sus -
pected of CTE should be managed symptomatically based on 
their clinical presentation.84
Functional Evaluation and Treatment 
Concepts in Rehabilitation
In addition to the medical issues described above, some functional 
difficulties commonly assessed and treated after TBI by the reha -
bilitation team are highlighted below.
Vestibular Dysfunction
Individuals with both mild and severe TBI often present with 
complaints of dizziness and imbalance. The incidence of such 
symptoms is reported to be between 30% and 60% in TBI 
populations 217 and could be as high as 100% if the individual 
has sustained a temporal bone fracture. There are many etiolo -
gies for these symptoms. Injury to the peripheral nervous sys -
tem may result in vestibular symptoms with or without hearing 
loss. Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo is the most common 
cause for complaints of dizziness and imbalance. It is clinically 
characterized by brief episodes of vertigo provoked by move -
ment of the head. Though this can resolve with time, treat -
ments with canalith repositioning procedures can shorten the 
length of symptoms. 257 Another peripheral vestibular injury 
includes labyrinthine concussion, or the sudden onset of hear -
ing loss and vertigo after TBI in the absence of temporal bone 
fracture. These individuals are good candidates for vestibular 
and balance rehabilitation. T emporal bone fractures can lead to 
disruption of the bony labyrinth or internal auditory canal. 
T rauma to the external canal clinically manifests with bloody 
otorrhea and severe pain and must be differentiated from a 
traumatic tympanic membrane perforation.
Central causes of dizziness can include direct trauma to the 
brainstem and/or cerebellum. These lesions are often associated 
with typical ocular motor abnormalities on neurologic exams. 
These include various forms of nystagmus at rest, eye movement 
abnormalities resulting in diplopia, and abnormalities of pupillary 
response. Rehabilitation techniques to promote alternative strate-
gies for gaze stability can be successful to induce long-term adap -
tation in the CNS.258 The focus of such rehabilitation programs is 
to decrease symptoms and allow patients to be more active. In 
patients with postural instability, exercises to promote static and 
dynamic postural stability are conducted by manipulating visual, 
vestibular, and somatosensory cues in the environment.549
Visual/Perceptual Dysfunction
TBI can lead to many symptoms related to vision such as diplopia, 
photophobia, difficulties with tracking, and fixation and visuoper-
ceptual complaints. Injury may occur to the optic nerve, visual 
cortex, visual processing centers, or oculomotor nerves.526 Each of 
these areas has variable recovery patterns and rehabilitation inter-
ventions. Common findings observed with physiatric examination 
include oculomotor abnormalities, visual field deficits, and defi -
ciencies in visual acuity. The most common cranial nerve palsy 
after TBI is third nerve palsy. On examination, exotropia, ptosis, 
and mydriasis are observed on the affected side. Fourth cranial 
nerve palsy results in difficulty with convergence or near vision. 
Clinically, a head tilt may be noted. Sixth cranial nerve palsy is 
associated with difficulty of abduction of the affected eye, resulting 
in diplopia on lateral vision to that side. 665 Visual acuity can be 
affected by traumatic optic neuropathy, which is quite rare in TBI, 
or by direct injury to the occipital cortex, causing cortical visual 
impairment. Visual acuity can be tested in clinic with a Snellen eye 
chart.177 The visual fields can be restricted by injury to the optic 
nerve or the visual pathway, causing various patterns of quadran -
tanopia and hemianopia. The visual fields can be assessed infor -
mally with confrontation by presenting objects or the hand in each 
quadrant in alternation while the patient fixates on a central 
object, usually the examiner.86
A bedside visual field examination should be done as well as 
detailed functional testing by an OT . Attention must be sufficient 
to participate fully in this evaluation. A neuroophthalmologic 
evaluation can assist therapists in evaluating the impact of vision 
on ADLs and perceptual motor function. In a study of patients 
referred for evaluation due to visual complaints after TBI, 85% 
had visual acuity of 20/20 but 33% had cranial nerve palsy on 
examination.525 Vision therapy is the nonsurgical treatment 
option for visual disorders. An ophthalmologist, optometrist, or 
OT may prescribe and supervise a regimen of individually 
planned activities based on the physical examination. T reatment

Acute Prognostic Indicators of Outcome: A 
Clinical and Translational Research 
Perspective
Although comprehensive, validated statistical models are still in 
development, a considerable amount of research has evaluated how 
demographic, clinical, physiologic, biochemical, and genetic fac -
tors contribute to mortality and outcome. Studies to date provide 
unique clinical evidence that can guide rehabilitation practitioners 
as they care for patients with TBI in the acute care setting and 
throughout rehabilitation. Demographic factors like gender, age, 
substance abuse, and violence are relevant factors when consider -
ing outcome. Also, severity scores that characterize the nature and 
extent of injury and other clinical, biochemical, and physiologic 
markers also have utility as predictors of TBI outcome.
Demographics
Sex
Female sex is considered to be a risk factor for worse outcome, 
particularly in studies that include mildly injured subjects in the 
analysis.176,635 Increased prevalence of postconcussive symptoms 
are often reported among adult women, 45,494 with one sports 
concussion study suggesting worse symptoms among women 
injured during the luteal phase of their menstrual cycle. 676 Large 
epidemiologic studies assessing sex and menopausal status on 
mortality after TBI have been mixed, with some studies showing 
that menopausal women are at greater risk than younger pre -
menopausal women and other studies suggesting that menopausal 
status is protective. 51,134,467 In contrast, experimental TBI model 
studies suggest that female sex hormones can be neuroprotective 
against many biochemical mechanisms of secondary injury, 
including cerebral edema, excitotoxicity, oxidative injury, and 
inflammation.517 Previous clinical reports show that women with 
severe TBI may have an advantage over men with respect to some 
aspects of TBI pathophysiology.176,626,641 However, severe TBI has 
a profound effect clinically on endogenous serum hormone pro -
files. Elevations in serum hormones like estradiol measured early 
after injury are linked to greater mortality and worse global out -
come, regardless of sex. 498,502,632 This pattern, along with CNS/
serum cortisol elevations and inflammation,338,533,534 likely reflects 
the systemic response to the injury and its effects on the auto -
nomic nervous system and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis 
activation and may contribute to nonneurologic organ dysfunc -
tion (NNOD). NNOD is commonly observed with critical ill -
ness, including roughly a third of those with severe TBI, and 
carries a significant increase (63%) in odds of mortality during 
acute hospitalization.297,701 Recent work suggests potential causal 
effects between acute serum estradiol and TNF- a, as well as age-
dependent sex differences, on NNOD (particularly cardiovascular 
dysfunction),336 which may contribute to the varied outcomes 
observed in the large cohort studies mentioned.
Age
TBI in the elderly is more commonly the result of falls than in 
younger age groups. Fragile bridging veins in the elderly contribute 
to higher incidence of SDH in this age group. Underlying medical 
conditions such as heart arrhythmias and artificial valves that 
require artificial anticoagulation increase the propensity for intra-
cranial hemorrhage with falls in this demographic group. Decreased 
balance and syncopal episodes from a variety of causes also con -
tribute to falls and TBI in the elderly. Neuronal loss that occurs 
with increasing age likely also influences fall incidence. Age-related 
mitochondrial dysfunction associated with neurodegenerative dis-
eases likely influences the secondary biochemical injury response 
observed in older patients with TBI. 80 In vitro  studies show  
that hippocampal vulnerability to excitotoxic injury increases as a  
function of age and is related to a more pronounced loss of intra-
cellular Ca 11  homeostasis and mitochondrial dysfunction. 377 
More oxidative stress has been noted for a given excitotoxic or 
ischemic insult in older adults with TBI.622 Also, increasing age is 
associated with a higher sex and stress hormone response and 
inflammation after TBI, both of which may contribute to NNOD-
associated mortality.338,534,632 For older individuals with TBI, there 
is greater acute care resource consumption per favorable outcome 
than in their younger counterparts. 475 Rehabilitation discharge 
outcomes are often similar for older and younger individuals with 
TBI, but the length of IPR and hospital costs needed to attain 
similar functional gains are greater for older patients.106
Violence and Substance Use
Intentional or violent TBI carries a higher mortality rate than 
other accidental mechanisms of injury.636 Among survivors, there 
are no differences in functional assessments after injury, but vic -
tims of intentional injury report lower levels of community  
integration and function. 636 Alcohol and illegal substances are 
commonly observed in the blood at the time of injury and can 
contribute to acute complications, longer hospitalizations, and 
worse outcomes.116
Clinical Variables
The GCS590 is crucial in determining severity of neurologic injury 
after trauma and is used with other variables to predict outcome. 
The Revised T rauma Score and Injury Severity Score (ISS) are 
effective predictors of discharge disposition across a broad popula-
tion hospitalized with TBI. 636 Revised T rauma Score and GCS 
have been used to predict disability and community integration 
1 year after injury in this population. 628 Coma duration is linked 
with injury severity and outcome, and individuals with coma 
greater than 2 weeks are unlikely to have a good recovery. 72 PTA 
duration is associated with DAI and is a strong predictor of func-
tional outcome.294,691 It has been reported that individuals with 
less than 2 weeks of PTA often have a good recovery, while those 
in PTA for greater than 4 weeks have a much smaller likelihood 
of good recovery.293 Recent multisite evidence also suggests that 
individuals undergoing craniectomy or craniotomy during acute 
care have worse global outcomes and supervision needs 2 years 
after injury compared to a matched sample. 296
Radiologic indices from CT studies have some utility in pre -
dicting outcomes. DAI leads to cerebral atrophy 144 and is associ -
ated with coma and PTA, both of which are associated with 
poorer long-term outcomes. T raumatic SAH (tSAH) has been 
associated with worse injury and poorer outcomes compared to 
other types of lesions,384 and tSAH can be graded to predict out -
come.234 However, it is unclear whether tSAH is simply a marker 
of more severe TBI, or if it adversely impacts outcome through 
direct mechanisms like vasospasm and ischemia. 25 Midline shift 
reflects elevated ICP and contributes to poor outcomes while

individuals who have EDH evacuated often have relatively good 
outcomes.366
EEG data have been classified in several ways for the purposes 
of prognosticating outcome. Some evidence suggests that the 
presence of more normal wake-sleep patterns, including the pres-
ence of sleep spindles, is indicative of better outcomes. 70 EEG 
characteristics of coma considered favorable for survival are usu -
ally sensitive to external stimuli and include relatively normal 
activity, dominant and rhythmic theta activity, frontal rhythmic 
delta activity, and spindle coma patterns present where stage two 
sleep patterns most often appear. Individuals with posttraumatic 
coma also exhibit burst suppression patterns when cerebral anoxia 
results secondarily from the injury. Additional negative prognostic 
characteristics involve nonreactive alpha pattern coma with alpha 
range activity predominant in the anterior regions or broadly 
distributed throughout the cortex.576 Reduction in the percentage 
of alpha variability (PAV), a quantitative EEG (QEEG) parame -
ter, also signifies a poor prognosis. 601 However, the most severe 
malignant factor for survival is isoelectric EEG activity.576
Biomarkers
Biomarkers are utilized by mainstream clinical care to quantita -
tively assess and define injury/disease in almost every organ sys -
tem other than the brain.49 However, biomarkers have the poten-
tial to assist clinicians with TBI diagnosis, prognosis, and 
treatment and management strategy development. Although bio-
markers are traditionally defined as protein measurements in 
biofluids, genes also represent a rich biomaterial for identifying 
individualized data for how a person may respond to an injury or 
treatment, as well as degree of risk for a particular complication 
after injury. As TBI biomarker research has increased in recent 
years, best practices for sample collection and biorepository man-
agement have been issued by the National Institutes of Health, 
National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke,448 along 
with the common data elements ( https://www.commondataele-
ments.ninds.nih.gov/T raumatic%20Brain%20Injury), in order to 
facilitate translation of findings to clinical care. The concept of 
rehabilomics has evolved over the past several years as a rehabili -
tation-relevant biomarker-based research model. 618,620,642 In the 
area of TBI and associated NNOD, the humoral triad of acute 
and chronic neurotrophin, hormone, and inflammation biomark-
ers is linked with multimodal domains of function and biosuscep-
tibility to complications. 631 These research programs give rise to 
the possibility of personalized biologic and other treatments to 
address the many conditions observed in individuals dealing with 
chronic TBI.619,640
Proteomics. Biomarkers identified in TBI often represent 
CNS structural damage associated with the injury. These bio -
markers are derived from the major cell types in the CNS. Each 
of these structural damage biomarkers also has prognostic capac -
ity in adults with TBI. 198 However, humoral markers, such as 
cytokines, hormones, and trophic factors, also carry some prog -
nostic capacity, including in the postacute phase.631
S100b is a calcium-binding protein present in high concentra-
tions in astrocytes, and elevated S100b levels indicate irreversible 
astrocyte damage.317 Myelin basic protein (MBP) is abundant in 
myelin. As such, elevated MBP levels represent axonal injury. 259 
NSE is a glycolytic enzyme localized predominantly in neuronal 
cytoplasm, with elevated levels reflective of neuronal injury. In 
addition to its diagnostic potential in mild TBI, serum S100b 
levels have been linked with impending mortality in severely 
injured patients, increased ICP , and global outcomes such as 
GOS.49,229 NSE is linked to neuroinflammation 487 as well as low 
GCS and poor outcome in adults and children with reasonable 
sensitivity and specificity.50,239 Initial NSE levels in particular have 
been helpful in outcome prognostication for both adults and 
children.50,413 Both S100b and NSE absolute levels and duration 
of elevation have been linked to deficits with neuropsychological 
testing.261 MBP also has been linked with both mortality and 
outcomes. 49,681
In the realm of TBI, the early detection and application of 
biomarkers such as GFAP , UCH-L1, and microtubule-associated 
protein 2 (MAP-2) have shown significant promise in improving 
clinical outcomes and guiding treatment strategies. These bio -
markers can be detected within 30 to 60 minutes postinjury, 
providing insights into the presence of intracranial lesions and the 
need for neurosurgical interventions. 130,141,322,655 GFAP , in par-
ticular, has demonstrated strong independent associations with 
TBI severity, with elevated levels indicating a higher likelihood of 
traumatic intracranial lesions and early mortality. 470 This bio -
marker, approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) 
for assessing mild TBI, serves as a reliable tool for early risk 
stratification, potentially aiding in decisions regarding patient 
transport and urgent care in various settings, such as trauma cen -
ters and military facilities. 602,608 MAP-2, a newer marker, also 
shows potential in enhancing prognostic models for TBI.195,385,579 
Neurofilament light (NfL) serves as a promising biomarker for 
TBI, correlating with injury severity, and can be measured nonin-
vasively in serum, reflecting neuroaxonal damage. Unlike GFAP , 
acute serum NfL levels continue to rise over the first week after 
moderate-to-severe injury and peak 20 to 30 days postinjury, with 
both week 1 levels and month 2 to 6 levels significantly associated 
with global outcome and DRS scores. 647 Elevated neurofilament 
levels are linked to poorer outcomes and can help monitor recov-
ery in patients with TBI 547 as well as gauge cognitive perfor -
mance.599 Interestingly, chronic elevations in p-tau, a key marker 
associated with CTE, recently have been associated with global 
outcomes and disability in a rehabilitation-relevant population 
with TBI.524 Taken together, the integration of these biomarkers 
into clinical practice could not only enhance TBI classification 
efforts but also revolutionize TBI management, offering a robust 
framework for acute care and also rehabilitation-focused clinical 
decisions and the design of future therapeutic trials.
Most CNS biomarkers found in serum after TBI remain there 
only transiently. Thus very little research has focused on identify-
ing biomarker profiles obtained during the rehabilitation or 
chronic phases of recovery. However, recent work suggests that 
inflammatory load is elevated for individuals who are months 
after TBI and is associated with global outcomes 616 as well as 
cognitive performance. 424 Recent studies also show persistent 
reductions in BDNF may impact cognitive and depressive symp -
toms over the first year postinjury. 170 Further work suggests 
chronic sex hormone derangements over the first year after msTBI 
for both men and women are likely due to long-term problems 
with pituitary function.40,501
The literature suggests that multiple molecular targets are asso-
ciated with key biochemical pathways involved with secondary 
injury mechanisms that lead to tissue damage and may also have 
utility as biomarkers in prognosticating outcomes and monitoring 
treatment response. The majority of studies evaluating these types 
of markers have utilized CSF or extracellular fluid (ECF) from 
human brain tissue obtained during the first week after injury to 
examine elevations or decreases in biomarkers clinically in patients 
with severe TBI. CNS glutamate levels are elevated early after

TBI,680,694 a phenomenon associated with poor outcome.227 Addi-
tionally, sex influences CSF glutamate levels over time, and treat-
ments like therapeutic hypothermia can significantly reduce levels 
of this excitatory neurotransmitter in a sex-specific manner. 626 
Similarly, F2-isoprostane, a marker of oxidative injury, has been 
associated with outcomes 622 as well as sex-specific response to 
hypothermia.43 CSF levels of the highly oxidative neurotransmit -
ter DA are influenced by both sex and genetics as well as acute 
vasopressor administration.634 Mitochondrial markers associated 
with apoptosis-like cytochrome-C and BCL-2 have been linked 
with both injury type and outcome. In children, elevated CSF 
cytochrome C levels had a strong association with inflicted TBI. 
In severely injured adults, persistently elevated CSF cytochrome-
C levels were associated with poorer 6-month GOS scores. 621 
High CSF concentrations of the prosurvival protein BCL-2 have 
also been linked with improved outcomes in children with severe 
TBI108 but with poor outcomes in adults. 621 These differences in 
BCL-2 outcomes may be the result of inherent differences in CNS 
apoptosis across age groups. Cytoskeletal proteins like a II spec-
trin and its breakdown products482 and both pro- and antiinflam-
matory markers551 in the CSF have been linked with survival and 
outcome. CSF inflammatory burden can reflect extracranial 
injury, which also impacts outcome.333,551
Genomics. Apolipoprotein E (APOE) has been studied ex -
tensively with regard to its role in plasma lipoprotein transport. 
There are multiple isoforms of APOE, with functional differ -
ences in plasma lipoprotein transport that occur as a result of 
these isoforms. Numerous studies have linked the expression of 
the APOE4 isoform with an increased risk of Alzheimer disease, 
and evidence suggests that TBI triggers ongoing degenerative 
pathology similar to that observed with Alzheimer dementia. As 
such, some genetic association studies in TBI pathophysiology 
and outcome have focused on APOE. Some studies suggest no 
association between the APOE4 allele and outcome, 422 while 
several other studies link APOE4 genotypes with worse func -
tional and cognitive outcomes. 12,122,165,488,592 Also, two large TBI 
studies evaluating possession of the APOE4 allele and outcome 
found a significant age-genotype interaction where adolescent/
young adults carrying the APOE4 allele had worse outcomes 
compared to a younger population without this allele. 165,591 The 
frequency of the APOE4 allele among individuals with b-
amyloid deposition is 52%, which is higher than reported in the 
Alzheimer population. However, in TBI subjects without b-
amyloid deposition, the APOE4 allelic frequency is 16% and 
similar to control subjects without Alzheimer disease. 452 Recent 
data suggest higher frequency of the APOE4 allele among post-
9/11 veterans exposed to close-range blast who demonstrate 
white matter damage 571 and experience cognitive deficits. 672 In 
addition, recent work implicates genetic variability in the pro -
moter region of the neprilysin gene, the primary enzyme respon -
sible for b-amyloid degradation and the incidence of Alzheimer 
type b-amyloid plaques in TBI. 282
While TBI pathophysiology and outcome have been linked, in 
part, to genetic variability with APOE, less is known about the 
role of other candidate genes in mediating outcomes post-TBI. 
BDNF genotype has been implicated in TBI global outcome.169,171 
Another study suggests that variability in the IL-1b gene influ -
ences global outcome in individuals with severe TBI 610 and also 
development of complications like PTE.139 Other genetic variants 
associated with glutamate transmission, adenosine pathways,  
and GABA neurotransmission also have been associated with 
PTE.133,140,334,510,633
There are several identified variants for a number of dopami -
nergic genes associated with cognitive and psychiatric conditions 
such as ADHD, depression, impulsivity, and Parkinson disease. 
Many symptoms associated with these diseases overlap with defi -
cits associated with TBI. A TaqIA restriction fragment length 
polymorphism for the D2 receptor affects D2 receptor levels and 
glucose metabolism in the brain. It is also linked with verbal 
memory performance after mild TBI392; this polymorphism influ-
ences multiple cognitive outcome domains 172 as well as behavior 
among TBI survivors screening positive for depression.442 Genetic 
variants within the vesicular monoamine transporter 2 gene are also 
associated with cognitive performance.376 Other work shows that 
variations in the promoter region of the DAT1 gene that affect 
gene transcription are linked with DAT receptor densities in the 
striatum chronically after TBI. 637 Importantly, one study has 
demonstrated the plausibility of cumulative genetic burden for 
risk variants within dopamine pathways to predict cognitive per -
formance in a sex-specific manner, with women being more  
susceptible than men to genetic burden–associated cognitive per -
formance deficits.441
Chromatin is a structure consisting of DNA plus interacting 
proteins called histones that help compact DNA in the nucleus 
and regulate gene expression. While the human genome repre -
sents genetic diversity derived from DNA, the epigenome is a 
composite of chromatin plus a pattern of chromatin modification 
that results from interactions between the chromatin and its envi-
ronment. Epigenetics include the transmission and perpetuation 
of information (i.e., heritable phenotypes unrelated to the DNA 
sequence) and is an important mechanism for the stable mainte -
nance of gene expression that involves physically marking DNA 
or histones. Epigenetic modification significantly affects learning 
and cognition in animal models. 345 Work in experimental TBI 
models shows that epigenetic modification of histones is reduced 
in the hippocampus after TBI, a finding that may contribute to 
learning and memory deficits. 205 Epigenetic modification also 
occurs with the application of an enriched environment, 189 an 
intervention that repeatedly has been shown to improve learning 
and memory in experimental TBI models. 248,311,630 Although 
chromatin remodeling studies have not yet been linked to clinical 
prognosis, epigenetic biomarkers may be a promising strategy for 
further insight into patient prognosis, treatment efficacy, and 
individualized environmental response to TBI.
Neuroimaging Modalities in Traumatic Brain 
Injury Research
Research in advanced neuroimaging techniques continues to 
develop rapidly, and their use in clinical medicine is increasingly 
accepted.668 Efforts to formulate common data elements for TBI 
have included recommendations for a tiered approach to imag -
ing data collection, with basic clinical imaging as a first tier and 
more advanced imaging added, depending on the area injured, 
severity of injury, and need for evaluation of changes over time. 
In addition, centers with additional resources and research capa -
bilities may collect data via more advanced imaging protocols in 
a standardized manner. This effort may allow for data to be 
compiled for testing and validation of imaging techniques in 
large groups across multiple centers, moving the science forward 
with greater efficiency of time and resources. Recent reviews on 
neuroimaging related to TBI describe current and emerging 
techniques.127,156

997Chapter 44  Traumatic Brain Injury
fMRI is arguably the most well-known type of neuroimaging 
used in neuroscience research, as it has been used widely to study 
cognitive processes in healthy individuals, with results published 
often in the media. Variation in local blood oxygen levels (blood 
oxygen level dependent [BOLD]) superimposed on high resolu -
tion imaging are interpreted as changes in cortical metabolic 
activity as a proxy for cortical neuronal activity.
97 BOLD fluctua-
tions can be examined at rest to depict the default mode network, 
for example, or with active challenges such as instructing the 
patient to imagine a motor task while being scanned. Studies that 
have been conducted consistently show differences in laterality of 
activation, as well as more widespread dispersion of activity for 
individuals with mild
96,393,394 and msTBI 103,476,538 compared to 
healthy controls ( Fig. 44.10). These patterns of increased activa -
tion and altered laterality occur, especially as task difficulty is 
increased, even when individuals with TBI are performing nor -
mally on the tasks presented.
394,476 These changes have been 
observed for simple motor tasks495 and cognitive tasks and suggest 
that individuals with TBI exert increased cognitive effort in order 
to perform at the same behavioral level as healthy individuals.
Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) adds directionality (fractional 
anisotropy) to the depiction of water molecule movement (mean 
diffusivity/apparent diffusion coefficient) afforded by DWI, pro -
viding information about axonal integrity.
271 Low fractional 
anisotropy is held to be representative of traumatic axonal injury 
(TAI), a type of diffuse axonal injury often not detected on  
CT and conventional MRI modalities.
273 A meta-analysis of 20 
studies identified a strong association between memory and/or 
attention and DTI pathology in the corpus callosum, fornix, 
internal capsule, and arcuate and uncinate fasciculi.
644 Diffusion 
tractography goes a step further to show neural pathways and 
associated disruptions after TBI, which has been used to under -
stand deficits of wakefulness and arousal.
Single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) uti -
lizes externally derived radioisotopes to evaluate resting blood 
flow and certain types of neuroreceptor binding. The dependent 
variable in SPECT derives from the well-established principle of 
increased cerebral activity correlating with increased blood flow.
523 
SPECT is quite sensitive in detecting regional differences in rest -
ing brain blood flow and has some clinical utility in mild TBI, 
and SPECT has detected abnormalities in resting blood flow in 
cases where CT results were normal.
228 SPECT also may have 
better prognostic ability than CT or MRI,451 in the sense that the 
number, size, and specific areas of reduced cerebral blood flow 
determined by SPECT appear to have greater predictive value 
regarding outcomes than MRI or CT findings. However, SPECT 
is less able to detect smaller lesions when compared to MRI. Also, 
because the tracers have half-lives of hours rather than minutes or 
seconds, SPECT is not useful for observing rapid changes in 
blood flow, such as what might occur with cognitive processing as 
measured by fMRI. SPECT images are also not as sensitive as 
PET and require imaging times longer than MRI.
The growing body of knowledge surrounding the clinical util-
ity of advanced imaging techniques, especially functional and 
metabolic modalities, continues to pose the question of when 
their use should be recommended in the routine management of

TBI of all severities. Currently, they remain research tools that see 
clinical use in extenuating circumstances, as wide discrepancies in 
institutional resources represent significant obstacles to dissemi -
nated use. Efforts will be required by experts in the field in order 
to evaluate and address these issues.221
Community Integration
Community integration is the ultimate goal for individuals with 
TBI and includes self-care, mobility, and physical function, as 
well as participation in vocational, social, and community 
roles.527 Common tools to assess community integration follow -
ing TBI include the Community Integration Questionnaire 
(CIQ),527 Craig Hospital Assessment and Reporting T echnique 
(CHART),656 and Community Integration Measure (CIM). 395 
The CIQ and CHART are objective measures of community 
integration through observation of behaviors and activities. The 
CIM assesses community integration from the individual’s per -
spective. These tools focus on the subjective individual experi -
ence regarding their functional ability and personal autonomy 
instead of objective behaviors associated with integration. CIMs 
are collected through self-report and can be collected by proxy. 
However, on the CIQ, proxy reports can be different from self-
report.129 Measurements specific to the examining of community 
integration in the TBI population are the CIQ and CIM. Online 
versions of the CIQ and CHART are located at http://www.
tbims.org/combi/list.html.
Vocational Rehabilitation
Return to employment after TBI can increase an individual’s per-
ceived QoL, social integration within the community, and home 
and leisure activities.460 However, individuals with TBI may expe-
rience cognitive, emotional, and/or physical deficits after injury 
that limit their ability to return to prior employment or school 
participation.373 In the United States, vocational rehabilitation is 
a state-supported service that aids individuals with disabilities in 
securing employment appropriate to their capabilities. State-  
specific departments providing vocational rehabilitation services 
can be found at https://askjan.org/. Vocational rehabilitation pro-
grams assess eligibility and vocational rehabilitation needs and 
provide counseling, vocational guidance, employment develop -
ment and training, assistive devices and/or technology, job place-
ment, coaching and follow-up, occupational licenses, tools and 
equipment, independent living services, and advocacy support 
(https://rsa.ed.gov/about/states). Vocational rehabilitation can 
either provide vocational services or refer clients to other agencies 
and programs that assist individuals with disabilities.
Family and Caregiver Resources
Rehabilitation practitioners play an important role in directing 
caregivers to education and community resource support services. 
There are many national and statewide organizations that exist to 
provide families and caregivers with education and community 
resources. Just a few examples of such organizations include the 
Brain Injury Association of America ( https://www.biausa.
org/), the Family Caregiver Alliance ( https://www.caregiver.
org/), the Caregiver Action Network (https://caregiveraction.
org/), the National Alliance for Caregiving ( https://www.care -
giving.org/ ), the Rosalyn Carter Institute for Caregiving ( http://
www.rosalynncarter.org/), and the Well Spouse Association  
(https://wellspouse.org/). These organizations provide links and 
contact information for support programs and groups; work -
shops and seminars; care management and in-home health ser -
vices; respite programs; hospice and palliative care services; adult 
day programs; financial planning, insurance, and legal aid assis -
tance; and advocacy groups.
Driving
T ransportation is one of the most important links to community 
integration as a necessary component for access to work, social 
support, recreation, and health care utilization.657 However, driv-
ing is a complex cognitive task that requires skills including accu-
rate reaction time, motion perception, spatial orientation, divided 
attention, multitasking, planning, decision making, and self-
control. Additionally, driving comprises psychomotor compo -
nents that may be difficult for brain-injured individuals if they 
have experienced additional physical injuries such as limb frac -
tures or visual impairments. Between 40% and 60% of individu -
als with msTBI return to driving after their injury. 456 Individuals 
with TBI who do not return to driving are at increased risk of 
depression, decreased life satisfaction, and loss of identity, and 
they perceive social and emotional barriers, such as caregiver 
opposition, as greater than their physical impairments in not 
returning to driving.530 States vary on the rules and regulations on 
return to driving after an injury. Some states require individuals 
to be free of seizures for a period before they can resume driv -
ing.456 Some states require physicians to report to their state 
agency if an individual in their care is unsafe to drive. Other states 
require licensed drivers to report changes in medical status before 
they can resume driving.
A formal driving evaluation is recommended for individuals 
with TBI to assess their ability to return to driving. Evaluation 
and training should be conducted by professionals certified 
through the Association for Driver Rehabilitation Specialists. 
Evaluation consists of review of cognitive abilities and a test on 
vehicle operation. Driving simulators can assess return to driving 
potential prior to on-road tests. Also, driving simulators provide 
stimulation that may lead to improvements in complex neurocog-
nitive skills needed for on-road driving.353
Veterans
As an unprecedented number of military personnel have returned 
from recent conflicts having both diagnosed and undiagnosed 
TBI, the research and focus on clinical care related to brain 
injury continues to increase exponentially. Due to the nature of 
blast-related injuries, veterans have complex medical issues in 
addition to their TBI and require comprehensive rehabilitation. 
As discussed earlier, pain and mental health issues occur at high 
rates within this population. T reatment needs to include cogni -
tive-behavioral interventions, pain care, assistive devices, and 
mental health interventions. 537 The T raumatic Brain Injury  
Center of Excellence ( https://www.health.mil/Military-Health-
T opics/Centers-of-Excellence/T raumatic-Brain-Injury-Center-
of-Excellence) provides resources and services for veterans who 
have sustained a TBI as well as patient and family education. 
Additional resources for veterans include After Deployment 
(https://www.afterdeployment.org/), a mental wellness resource, 
and Hire Vets ( https://www.hirevets.gov/), which provides 
employers with the tools needed to help returning veterans with 
TBI succeed in the workplace.

999Chapter 44 Traumatic Brain Injury
Community Services
Community re-entry and integration is an important goal for 
individuals who have experienced TBI. Community services are 
available to assist them and their caregivers in this goal. Brain 
injury support groups provide TBI individuals and family mem -
bers or caregivers a forum where they can increase their knowledge 
of brain injury issues, provide emotional support to each other, 
network, and learn from others who have had similar experiences. 
State brain injury affiliates can provide information on support 
groups and locations (https://www.biausa.org/find-bia). The Brain 
Injury Association provides an online national directory of services 
by state. Community services include community re-entry pro -
grams, community-based programs, day treatments, transitional 
living, independent living, and recreation programs. A national 
directory of services for people with TBI can be found at https://
www.brainline.org/resource-directory/national.
Traumatic Brain Injury Prevention
Primary TBI prevention focuses on the prevention of injuries 
through policy and legislation while secondary prevention focuses 
on decreasing the harm of brain injuries as they occur. T ertiary 
prevention addresses care management and rehabilitation of those 
already injured to further reduce consequences of the injury. 
Effective TBI prevention relies on systemic and individual actions 
and precautions.
Helmet use is one of the most important aspects of TBI pre -
vention. Helmets attenuate the impact of energy and distribute 
this impact force applied to the head.399 The TBI Act of 2008 was 
enacted to provide standards for surveillance of the incidence and 
prevalence of TBI in the United States as well as contribute to 
expansion and improvement of TBI programs. 2 In 1995, Con -
gress issued a provision of the Intermodal Surface T ransportation 
Efficiency Act that affected motorcycle helmet use. Several states 
loosened or repealed their universal helmet laws, which had previ-
ously required helmet use for all riders. Following this policy 
change, helmet use nationally declined. The change in policy has 
affected TBI rates; individuals from states without universal hel -
met laws are 41% more likely to sustain severe TBI than those 
from states with these laws.111 In another example of primary TBI 
prevention related to helmet use, American football and ice 
hockey organizations have implemented the mandatory use of 
protective helmets as a means of decreasing the incidence of head 
injuries. The implementation of helmet standards in American 
football by the National Operating Committee on Standards for 
Athletic Equipment, in addition to regulations controlling head 
blocking and tackling, has resulted in a 74% decrease in fatalities 
in the sport as well as an 84% reduction in TBI in the sport since 
1976.351,352 Multiple rule changes in the National Football  
League in 2018, including the “targeting” rule that penalizes 
blows directed to the head, were correlated with a decreased  
incidence of concussion in the ensuing 5 years, 386 and practice 
strategies that limited contact decreased concussions suffered in 
practice by 64%. 161 In youth ice hockey, policies that disallow 
bodychecking were associated with a 58% decreased incidence of 
concussion.161
The individual act of wearing a helmet during a potentially 
dangerous activity is an example of secondary prevention. Motor-
cycle and bicycle helmets are effective methods for head injury 
prevention. Motorcycle helmets are 65% effective in preventing 
TBI.390 Bicycle helmets reduce brain injury by 88% and severe 
TBI by 75%. 597 In sport, head injuries most often occur in 
American football, rugby, ice hockey, baseball, soccer, cricket, 
horse racing, and skiing/snowboarding. Although helmet use is 
effective in preventing serious TBI, it is unknown if helmets pre -
vent concussions.399 Although initial data were less clear,314 more 
recent meta-analyses associate mouthguard use with a 28% 
decreased incidence of concussion in ice hockey. 161 Helmets also 
should be worn in sports even when their use is not mandatory. 
Ski helmets can reduce the incidence of TBI by 56%, though 
there is the potential for ski helmets to increase the risk of neck 
injury.240 In baseball, softer balls can reduce the risk of injury by 
28% in comparison with standard balls. 379 When evaluating hel-
mets in the safe use of recreational vehicles (i.e., bicycles, motor -
cycles, all-terrain vehicles, skateboards, scooters), individuals 
without helmets had more abnormal neuroimaging studies and 
more frequent ICU visits.204 Research also is being conducted to 
determine how differences in ground surface, that is, grass versus 
artificial surfaces, affect TBI rates in sports.449
Common automobile safety features that decrease injury include 
seat belts and air bags.483 New advances in automotive features that 
may contribute to decreases in injury include tire-pressure monitor-
ing systems, electronic stability control, lane-departure warning, 
rollover prevention, adaptive headlights, rearview cameras, and 
emergency response systems. 427 Automatic collision notification 
technology can provide call centers with the exact location of 
vehicle crashes so appropriate personnel can be dispatched to the 
scene with faster response times and more efficient and timely care 
for individuals affected.153
As this chapter highlights, rehabilitative care focuses on con -
taining the injury process and preventing complications. Compre-
hensive transdisciplinary rehabilitation programs are necessary for 
individuals with TBI to prevent further injury and return to an 
optimal level of function within the limits imposed by their injury. 
T ranslational research is important to enhance the ability to pro-
vide novel, personalized, and effective rehabilitation interventions.
